questions.qText: Explain Big O notation with examples.

blocks.text: Big O describes the upper bound of time/space growth as input size n grows. We care about the dominant term and ignore constants.

O(1) - constant: array access by index, HashMap get/put. O(log n) - logarithmic: binary search. O(n) - linear: single loop. O(n log n) - merge sort, heap sort. O(n^2) - nested loop: bubble sort. O(2^n) - exponential: naive Fibonacci recursion. O(n!) - factorial: brute-force permutations.

questions.qText: When to use ArrayList vs LinkedList?

blocks.text: ArrayList for random access and most general use - get/set by index is O(1), iteration is faster due to cache locality. LinkedList ONLY if you do many insertions/deletions at the head or have a known iterator position. In practice, ArrayList wins almost always. For queue operations, prefer ArrayDeque over LinkedList.

questions.qText: Time complexity of HashMap operations?

blocks.text: Average case: O(1) for get, put, remove, containsKey. Worst case (many collisions, all keys hash to same bucket): O(n) before Java 8, O(log n) since Java 8 (chain converts to Red-Black tree at threshold 8). For interview purposes, assume O(1) average.

questions.qText: How does binary search work? Implement it.

blocks.text: Binary search works on a SORTED array. Repeatedly narrow the search range by comparing the middle element. Time: O(log n). Space: O(1) iterative, O(log n) recursive.
Pitfall: int mid = (low + high) / 2 can overflow for large values. Always use low + (high - low) / 2.

questions.qText: What is dynamic programming? When to use it?

blocks.text: DP is solving complex problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing results to avoid recomputation.
Use DP when: 1) The problem has optimal substructure (optimal solution built from optimal sub-solutions). 2) Subproblems overlap (same sub-problem solved many times in naive recursion).

Two approaches: Memoization (top-down: recursion + cache) and Tabulation (bottom-up: fill a table iteratively). Tabulation is usually faster (no recursion overhead) but memoization is more intuitive for some problems.

questions.qText: DFS vs BFS - when to use which?

blocks.text: DFS (Depth First Search): goes as deep as possible before backtracking. Uses stack (recursion). Better for: finding any path, detecting cycles, topological sort, problems with all possibilities (backtracking).
BFS (Breadth First Search): explores level by level. Uses queue. Better for: shortest path in UNWEIGHTED graphs, level-order traversal, minimum steps problems, 'rotting oranges' style problems.
For shortest path in WEIGHTED graph, use Dijkstra (BFS with priority queue) or Bellman-Ford.

questions.qText: How does merge sort work?

blocks.text: Merge sort uses divide-and-conquer. 1) Divide: split array into two halves. 2) Conquer: recursively sort each half. 3) Combine: merge the two sorted halves.
Time complexity: O(n log n) - guaranteed, worst case too. Space: O(n) for the temporary merge array.
Pros: Stable sort, predictable performance. Cons: Extra space. Used in Java's Collections.sort() for objects (TimSort, a hybrid of merge sort and insertion sort).

questions.qText: How does quick sort work?

blocks.text: Quick sort: 1) Choose a pivot. 2) Partition: arrange so elements < pivot are on the left, > pivot on the right. 3) Recursively sort both partitions.
Time: O(n log n) average, O(n^2) worst case (already sorted with bad pivot choice). Space: O(log n) for recursion stack.

Pros: In-place, very fast in practice, cache-friendly. Cons: Not stable, worst case O(n^2). Pivot choice matters (random or median-of-three). Used in Java's Arrays.sort() for primitives (Dual-Pivot Quicksort).

questions.qText: What is recursion? When does it cause stack overflow?

blocks.text: Recursion is when a method calls itself with a smaller subproblem until a base case is reached. Each call adds a frame to the call stack. Stack overflow occurs when: 1) Missing or unreachable base case (infinite recursion). 2) Recursion depth exceeds stack size (Java default is around 512KB - roughly 5000-10000 frames depending on frame size). Solution: use iteration, or convert to tail recursion (Java doesn't optimize this), or increase stack size with -Xss flag.

questions.qText: Explain Two Pointer technique.

blocks.text: Two pointer technique uses two indices traversing the array, often from opposite ends or at different speeds. Common patterns: 1) Opposite ends moving inward (e.g., Two Sum on sorted array, Container With Most Water). 2) Fast and slow pointers (e.g., detect cycle in linked list - Floyd's algorithm). 3) Sliding window (both pointers move forward, maintaining a window). Reduces O(n^2) brute force to O(n) for many problems.

questions.qText: Explain sliding window technique.

blocks.text: Sliding window is a pattern for problems involving contiguous subarrays/substrings. Maintain a window [left, right] and slide it through the array. Two variants: 1) Fixed size window - both pointers move together. 2) Variable size window - expand right to include, shrink left to satisfy a constraint. Examples: longest substring without repeating chars, minimum window substring, maximum sum of k consecutive elements. Reduces nested-loop O(n^2) brute force to O(n).

questions.qText: What is a Trie? When to use it?

blocks.text: Trie (prefix tree) stores strings character by character. Each node represents a character; paths from root form words. Used for: prefix-based search (autocomplete), spell check, dictionary lookups, longest common prefix, IP routing. Operations: insert and search are O(L) where L is the word length. Space: O(total characters across all words), but shared prefixes save space.

questions.qText: What is Union-Find (DSU)?

blocks.text: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) is a data structure that tracks elements partitioned into disjoint sets. Operations: find(x) - get representative of x's set; union(x, y) - merge two sets. Optimizations: path compression (flatten tree during find) and union by rank/size. With both, operations are nearly O(1) (inverse Ackermann). Used for: Kruskal's MST, cycle detection in undirected graph, connected components, network connectivity problems.

questions.qText: How to detect a cycle in a linked list?

blocks.text: Floyd's Cycle Detection (tortoise and hare): use two pointers, slow (moves 1 step) and fast (moves 2 steps).
If there's a cycle, the fast pointer will eventually catch up to the slow pointer inside the cycle. If fast reaches null, no cycle.
To find the start of the cycle: after they meet, reset one pointer to head and move both 1 step at a time. They meet at the cycle start. Time O(n), Space O(1).

questions.qText: Difference between BFS and DFS in terms of space complexity?

blocks.text: BFS: O(w) where w is the maximum width of the tree/graph. For a balanced binary tree of n nodes, BFS uses O(n) for the queue at the widest level. DFS: O(h) where h is the height. For a balanced tree, DFS uses O(log n). For a skewed tree, DFS can use O(n). For graphs, BFS uses O(V) for the queue and visited set; DFS uses O(V) for the recursion stack and visited set.

questions.qText: How would you find Kth largest element in an array?

blocks.text: Three approaches: 1) Sort the array, return arr[n-k]. Time O(n log n). Simple but slow.
2) Min-heap of size k: iterate through array, add to heap, remove smallest if size > k. After processing, root is Kth largest. Time O(n log k), space O(k). Best for streaming data.
3) Quickselect (partition-based): like quicksort but only recurse into one side. Average O(n), worst O(n^2). Best for one-time computation.

questions.qText: Explain topological sort.

blocks.text: Topological sort orders vertices of a directed acyclic graph (DAG) such that for every edge u -> v, u comes before v. Only works on DAGs - if there's a cycle, no valid topological order exists.
Two algorithms: 1) Kahn's algorithm (BFS): repeatedly remove nodes with in-degree 0. 2) DFS-based: do DFS, push to stack when done; reverse stack at the end.
Use cases: course prerequisite ordering, build systems, package managers, task scheduling. Time O(V + E).

questions.qText: How to find shortest path in a weighted graph?

blocks.text: Dijkstra's algorithm: works for non-negative weights. Greedy with min-heap. Time O((V + E) log V) with priority queue.
Bellman-Ford: handles negative weights, detects negative cycles. Time O(V * E).
Floyd-Warshall: all-pairs shortest path. Time O(V^3). Used when you need shortest distance between every pair.
For UNWEIGHTED graph, BFS gives shortest path in O(V + E).

questions.qText: What is memoization vs tabulation?

blocks.text: Memoization: top-down recursive approach where results of subproblems are cached (in HashMap or array). Easy to write by adding cache to naive recursion. Tabulation: bottom-up iterative approach where you fill a table starting from base cases. Usually faster (no recursion overhead, better cache locality) and can sometimes be space-optimized to O(1).

questions.qText: How to handle questions you don't know in a DSA interview?

blocks.text: Don't pretend. Use the structured approach: 1) Restate the problem in your own words. 2) Ask clarifying questions (input range, edge cases, expected output).

3) Discuss brute force first. Get any working solution. 4) Identify bottleneck, propose optimization. 5) Code the solution, talk through it. 6) Test with examples including edge cases. 7) Discuss time and space complexity.
Interviewers care more about thought process than the final answer. Showing structured thinking and good communication often beats silently solving.

questions.qText: Explain the JavaScript event loop.

blocks.text: JavaScript is single-threaded but achieves concurrency via the event loop:
1) Call Stack: where currently-executing code runs.
2) Web APIs (browser) / libuv (Node): handle async tasks (setTimeout, fetch, file I/O).
3) Callback Queue (macrotasks): setTimeout, setInterval, I/O callbacks.
4) Microtask Queue: Promise.then callbacks, queueMicrotask, MutationObserver.
5) Event Loop: when call stack is empty, processes ALL microtasks first, then ONE macrotask, repeat.

lines: // Why: sync first (1,4), then microtask (3 from Promise), then macrotask (2)

questions.qText: Map vs Object - when to use which?

blocks.text: Object: simple key-value, keys are strings/Symbols, iteration order roughly insertion. Best for static, known-key structures. Map: keys of ANY type (objects too!), explicit insertion order, has size, easier iteration. Best for dynamic dictionaries, lookups. Map operations slightly faster for large/frequent additions/removals.

questions.qText: What is hoisting in JavaScript?

blocks.text: JavaScript moves declarations (NOT initializations) to the top of their scope during compilation. var declarations are hoisted with undefined value. function declarations are fully hoisted (callable before definition). let/const declarations are hoisted but uninitialized (TDZ - accessing throws ReferenceError).

lines: bar();  // TypeError: bar is not a function

blocks.text: Instead of attaching listener to each child, attach ONE to a common ancestor. Use event.target to know which child triggered. Works because events bubble up. Benefits: less memory, works with dynamically added elements.

questions.qText: Difference between var, let, and const?

blocks.text: var: function-scoped, hoisted to top with undefined initialization, can be redeclared and reassigned. Legacy - avoid.
let: block-scoped, hoisted but in 'temporal dead zone' until declaration, can be reassigned, cannot be redeclared in same scope.
const: block-scoped, same TDZ behavior, cannot be reassigned. For objects/arrays, the binding is constant but contents are mutable.

questions.qText: What is hoisting in JavaScript?

blocks.text: Hoisting is JavaScript's behavior of moving declarations to the top of their scope at compile time.
Function declarations: fully hoisted (you can call them before their line).
var: only the declaration is hoisted; the variable is undefined until the assignment line.
let, const, class: declarations are hoisted but in 'temporal dead zone' - accessing them before declaration throws ReferenceError.

questions.qText: What is closure? Give a use case.

blocks.text: A closure is when an inner function 'remembers' variables from its outer function's scope, even after the outer function has finished executing.

Use case: data privacy. Example: function makeCounter() { let count = 0; return () => ++count; }. The returned function still has access to count even though makeCounter has returned. Each call to makeCounter() creates a new count.
Also used in module patterns, function factories, partial application, event handlers preserving state.

questions.qText: Explain the event loop.

blocks.text: JavaScript is single-threaded. The event loop coordinates execution: 1) Call stack - synchronous code runs here. 2) Web APIs - browser handles setTimeout, fetch, DOM events. 3) Task queue (macrotask queue) - setTimeout, setInterval callbacks. 4) Microtask queue - Promise callbacks, queueMicrotask.

After every sync task completes, the event loop empties ALL microtasks first, then takes ONE macrotask, then microtasks again. So Promises resolve before setTimeout(0) even though both 'fire after current code'.

questions.qText: Difference between == and === ?

blocks.text: == does type coercion before comparing (e.g., '5' == 5 is true). === checks both type and value, no coercion (e.g., '5' === 5 is false). Always use === to avoid bugs from unexpected coercion. The only use case for == is checking 'value == null' which covers both null and undefined.

questions.qText: What is the 'this' keyword in JavaScript?

blocks.text: 'this' refers to the execution context - what's calling the function. 4 rules:
1) Default binding: in regular function call, this = global object (window in browser) or undefined in strict mode.
2) Implicit binding: obj.method() - this = obj.
3) Explicit binding: func.call(obj), func.apply(obj), func.bind(obj) - this = obj.
4) new binding: new Func() - this = new instance.
Arrow functions don't have their own 'this' - they inherit from enclosing scope. That's why arrow functions are great for callbacks but bad as methods.

questions.qText: Difference between map(), filter(), and reduce()?

blocks.text: map: transforms each element, returns new array of same length. [1,2,3].map(x => x*2) returns [2,4,6].
filter: keeps elements that pass a predicate, returns new array (smaller or equal). [1,2,3,4].filter(x => x%2 === 0) returns [2,4].
reduce: accumulates into a single value. [1,2,3].reduce((acc, x) => acc + x, 0) returns 6. Most powerful - can implement map and filter using reduce.

questions.qText: Difference between Promise and async/await?

blocks.text: They are the same thing - async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises. An async function always returns a Promise. await pauses execution until the awaited Promise settles. Benefits of async/await: code looks synchronous, easier to read, easier error handling with try/catch. Promises (.then/.catch chains) are still useful for parallel operations (Promise.all). Use async/await for sequential logic, Promise.all for parallel.

questions.qText: What are higher-order functions?

blocks.text: A higher-order function is a function that either takes another function as argument OR returns a function (or both). Examples: map, filter, reduce, setTimeout, addEventListener. JavaScript treats functions as first-class citizens - they can be passed around like values. Enables functional programming patterns.

questions.qText: What is event delegation?

blocks.text: Event delegation attaches a single event listener to a parent element, instead of attaching listeners to many children. When an event bubbles up, the parent's listener can determine which child triggered it via event.target. Benefits: better performance with many elements, works for dynamically added children, less memory. Example: one listener on a <ul> handles clicks for all <li> items, even ones added later.

questions.qText: Explain the difference between localStorage, sessionStorage, and cookies.

blocks.text: localStorage: 5-10 MB, persists until manually cleared, same-origin only, NOT sent with HTTP requests, sync API.
sessionStorage: 5-10 MB, cleared when tab closes, same-origin only, NOT sent with requests.

Cookies: 4 KB limit, expires based on Expires/Max-Age, sent with every HTTP request to that domain (overhead), can be httpOnly (JS can't read - security), can be secure (HTTPS only). Use for: auth tokens (httpOnly), server-needed state.

questions.qText: Difference between null and undefined?

blocks.text: undefined: variable declared but not assigned, or function with no return value, or accessing non-existent property. The JS engine produces these. null: explicitly assigned to indicate 'no value'. Developers assign these intentionally. typeof undefined === 'undefined', but typeof null === 'object' (historic JS bug). undefined == null is true, undefined === null is false.

questions.qText: What is debounce and throttle?

blocks.text: Both limit how often a function runs - useful for expensive operations on rapid events (scroll, resize, input).
Debounce: delays execution until N ms have passed since the LAST call. Example: search-as-you-type - wait until user stops typing for 300ms before sending API call.
Throttle: ensures function runs at most once every N ms. Example: scroll handler - update only once every 100ms regardless of how fast user scrolls.

questions.qText: Difference between interface and type in TypeScript?

blocks.text: Both define shape of objects. Interfaces can be reopened/extended (declaration merging), types cannot.
Types can represent unions, intersections, primitives, tuples - more flexible. Interfaces are designed for object shapes and classes.
Modern best practice: use 'interface' for public API contracts (libraries) and 'type' for everything else (unions, complex types). Either works for most cases - pick one and be consistent.

questions.qText: What is TypeScript generics? Why use them?

blocks.text: Generics let you write reusable code that works with multiple types while preserving type safety. Example: function identity<T>(arg: T): T { return arg; }. T is inferred or specified - identity<number>(5) returns number. Without generics, you'd use 'any' and lose type information. Used heavily in collections (Array<T>), Promises (Promise<T>), and React components (useState<T>).

questions.qText: Context API vs Redux vs Zustand.
questions.header: Stage 6 - React: Advanced Q&A with code examples

blocks.text: Context: built-in. Good for low-frequency global state (theme, auth user). Re-renders all consumers on value change - performance issue with frequent updates.
Redux: predictable state, time-travel debugging, middleware ecosystem. Boilerplate-heavy historically, but Redux Toolkit cleaned it up. Good for complex state with many actions.
Zustand: minimal boilerplate, no providers needed, selective subscriptions (re-render only components that use changed slice). Modern preferred choice for most apps.

questions.qText: React.memo, useMemo, useCallback - the full memoization picture.
questions.header: Stage 6 - React: Advanced Q&A with code examples

blocks.text: React.memo: HOC that memoizes a component - skips re-render if props are shallowly equal. useMemo: caches a value computed by an expensive function. useCallback: caches a function reference. Use sparingly - measure first. Premature optimization adds complexity and can even hurt perf if dependency comparison costs more than the work being skipped.

questions.qText: Server Components vs Client Components (Next.js / React 19).
questions.header: Stage 6 - React: Advanced Q&A with code examples

blocks.text: Server Components: run only on the server. Can directly access DB, secrets, file system. Cannot use state, effects, browser APIs, or event handlers. Lighter bundle (no JS shipped to client). Default in Next.js App Router. Client Components: marked with 'use client' directive. Run on server (SSR) AND client. Can use useState, useEffect, event handlers. Send JS to client. Pattern: use server components for static/data-fetching parts, client components only where interactivity is needed.

questions.qText: Common React performance issues and fixes.
questions.header: Stage 6 - React: Advanced Q&A with code examples

blocks.text: 1) Unnecessary re-renders: parent re-renders -> all children re-render. Fix: React.memo on children, stable callbacks via useCallback.
2) Heavy computations in render: move to useMemo or move out of component entirely.
3) Large lists: use virtualization (react-window, react-virtuoso) - only render visible rows.
4) Inline functions/objects as props create new references every render. Extract or memoize.
5) Large bundle: code-split with React.lazy + Suspense. Split by route at minimum.
6) Context re-renders everyone. Split contexts by update frequency, or use selectors (Zustand, Jotai).

questions.qText: What is the virtual DOM? How does it work?

blocks.text: Virtual DOM is an in-memory representation of the real DOM as JavaScript objects. React maintains a virtual DOM tree.

When state changes: 1) React creates a new virtual DOM tree. 2) Diffs it against the previous virtual DOM tree (reconciliation). 3) Calculates the minimum set of changes needed. 4) Updates only those parts of the real DOM (batch updates).
Why? Real DOM operations are expensive. Diffing in JS is much faster. Result: better performance, declarative UI, predictable updates.

questions.qText: Difference between state and props?

blocks.text: Props are inputs passed from parent to child - read-only inside the child. Used to make components configurable. State is internal data managed within a component - mutable via setter. When state changes, component re-renders. Rule: state for what the component owns and controls; props for what comes from outside. State should live in the lowest common parent of components that need it.

questions.qText: What are React hooks? Why introduced?

blocks.text: Hooks are functions that let you use state and lifecycle features in functional components - features previously only available in classes.

Introduced in React 16.8 (2019) because: 1) Class components had confusing lifecycle methods. 2) Logic was hard to reuse (HOCs and render props were clunky). 3) 'this' binding was painful. 4) Classes were hard to optimize.

Built-in hooks: useState, useEffect, useContext, useReducer, useRef, useMemo, useCallback, useImperativeHandle, useLayoutEffect, useDebugValue, useId, useTransition, useDeferredValue. Plus custom hooks for reusable logic.

questions.qText: Rules of hooks?

blocks.text: 1) Only call hooks at the top level - never inside loops, conditions, or nested functions. React relies on the order hooks are called to associate state with each. 2) Only call hooks from React function components or custom hooks (functions starting with 'use'). The eslint-plugin-react-hooks enforces these rules.

questions.qText: Explain useState and useEffect.

blocks.text: useState: returns [stateValue, setterFunction]. Calling setter triggers re-render with new state. Multiple useState calls are fine - React tracks them by call order.

useEffect: runs side effects after render. Syntax: useEffect(callback, dependencies). Empty array [] = run once after mount. No array = run after every render. [a, b] = run when a or b changes. Return a cleanup function to run before next effect or on unmount.
useEffect equivalents: componentDidMount + componentDidUpdate + componentWillUnmount combined.

questions.qText: Difference between useEffect and useLayoutEffect?

blocks.text: useEffect runs ASYNCHRONOUSLY after the browser paints. The user sees the rendered UI first, then effect runs. Better for most cases - doesn't block paint. useLayoutEffect runs SYNCHRONOUSLY after DOM mutations but before paint. Use when you need to measure DOM and update before user sees it (e.g., positioning a tooltip) to avoid flicker. Otherwise prefer useEffect.

questions.qText: What is useCallback vs useMemo?

blocks.text: Both memoize values across renders. useMemo memoizes a computed VALUE: const value = useMemo(() => expensiveCalc(a), [a]).
useCallback memoizes a FUNCTION reference: const fn = useCallback(() => doSomething(a), [a]). It's actually equivalent to useMemo(() => fn, [deps]).

When to use: pass to child wrapped in React.memo to prevent re-render (function reference would change every render otherwise), or as dependency of another hook to avoid infinite loops. Don't memoize everything - it adds overhead.

questions.qText: What is React.memo? When to use?

blocks.text: React.memo is a higher-order component that memoizes a component - skips re-render if props haven't changed (shallow compare). Use when: 1) Component re-renders often. 2) Component is expensive. 3) Props are usually the same between renders. Don't use everywhere - the shallow compare itself has cost. Combine with useCallback for function props and useMemo for object props to ensure references are stable.

questions.qText: What is prop drilling? How to avoid it?

blocks.text: Prop drilling is passing props through multiple levels of components that don't actually use the data, just to get it to a deep child. Makes refactoring hard. Solutions: 1) Context API - for theme, auth, locale (rarely changing global data). 2) State management library - Redux Toolkit, Zustand for complex shared state. 3) Component composition - pass children as props to invert the relationship. 4) Server state in React Query - cached globally automatically.

questions.qText: When to use Context vs Redux?

blocks.text: Context: small to medium apps, simple shared state (theme, auth user, language), low-frequency updates. Built-in, no extra library.
Redux Toolkit: large apps, complex state with many actions, time-travel debugging, middleware, predictable state changes via reducers, when state updates need to be auditable.
Modern alternatives: Zustand (simpler than Redux, less boilerplate), Jotai (atomic), React Query (for SERVER state - which is most of your 'state' in modern apps).

questions.qText: What are keys in React lists? Why important?

blocks.text: Keys help React identify which items changed, added, or removed in a list. Without keys, React has to compare entire trees - slow and can cause UI bugs.

Use a STABLE, UNIQUE identifier (database id, unique string). Don't use array index unless: 1) The list never reorders. 2) Items don't have stable IDs. Otherwise index causes input fields to associate with wrong items when list changes.

questions.qText: Controlled vs uncontrolled components?

blocks.text: Controlled: form data lives in React state, value is set via state, onChange updates state. React is the source of truth. Use for validation, conditional disabling, dynamic forms. Uncontrolled: form data lives in the DOM, accessed via useRef when needed. Less code but harder to validate dynamically. Best practice: controlled for most forms; uncontrolled for file inputs and one-off forms.

questions.qText: What is the difference between class and functional components?

blocks.text: Class components: ES6 classes extending React.Component. Have lifecycle methods (componentDidMount, etc.), use this.state and this.setState. Legacy in modern React.
Functional components: plain functions returning JSX. Use hooks for state and effects. Cleaner code, easier reuse via custom hooks, better performance optimizations available.
Modern React: functional components only. Existing class components in older codebases still work, but new code shouldn't use them.

questions.qText: How does React Redux work?

blocks.text: Redux has 3 pieces: 1) Store - single source of truth, holds entire app state. 2) Actions - plain objects describing what happened (type + payload). 3) Reducers - pure functions that take current state + action, return new state.
Flow: component dispatches action -> reducer computes new state -> store updates -> subscribed components re-render.

Modern: use Redux Toolkit which reduces boilerplate. createSlice generates reducers + actions automatically. useSelector reads state, useDispatch sends actions. RTK Query handles server state with caching.

questions.qText: What is reconciliation?

blocks.text: Reconciliation is React's algorithm to determine what changed between renders. When state changes, React creates a new virtual DOM tree and compares it to the previous tree. The diffing algorithm: 1) Different element types = full unmount and remount. 2) Same type = compare attributes/props, update only changed ones. 3) For lists, uses keys to match elements. The Fiber architecture (React 16+) allows this work to be split into chunks and interrupted, keeping the UI responsive.

questions.qText: Explain Server Components in Next.js 13+.

blocks.text: Server Components run only on the server and send rendered HTML/JSX to the client. They can directly access databases, files, server-only secrets. They send ZERO JavaScript for that component to the client - smaller bundles. Cannot use hooks, browser APIs, or event handlers. Client Components (marked 'use client') run on both server (initial render) and client (interactivity). Mix them: Server Components for data display, Client Components for interactive parts.

questions.qText: What is hydration in SSR?

blocks.text: Hydration is when the React app on the client 'takes over' the server-rendered HTML. Server sends static HTML for fast first paint. Then React loads, attaches event handlers, and makes the page interactive. The DOM that React builds must match the server's HTML exactly - mismatches cause warnings or broken UI. Common issue: rendering different content on server vs client (e.g., Date.now()) causes hydration mismatch.

questions.qText: What are React's lifecycle methods (mapped to hooks)?

blocks.text: Class lifecycle to hook equivalents:
componentDidMount -> useEffect(() => {}, [])
componentDidUpdate -> useEffect(() => {}) or useEffect(() => {}, [deps])
componentWillUnmount -> useEffect(() => { return () => cleanup(); }, [])
shouldComponentUpdate -> React.memo + useMemo + useCallback
getDerivedStateFromProps -> derive during render (don't sync state to props)

questions.qText: What is the difference between useState and useReducer?

blocks.text: useState: simple, independent state values. useReducer: complex state with multiple sub-fields, or when next state depends on previous state and multiple actions. useReducer pattern: useReducer(reducer, initialState) returns [state, dispatch]. You dispatch actions, reducer computes new state. Use useReducer when: state has 5+ fields, multiple update patterns, related state fields change together, easier to test logic separately.

questions.qText: How to prevent re-renders in React?

blocks.text: 1) React.memo on the child component (skips render if props unchanged via shallow compare).
2) useCallback for function props so reference is stable.
3) useMemo for object/array props so reference is stable.
4) State colocation - keep state as close as possible to where it's used, so changes don't ripple up.
5) Split components - a small component that re-renders often is cheaper than a big one.
6) Avoid creating objects/functions inline in JSX where possible. Don't over-optimize - measure first with React DevTools Profiler.

questions.qText: Optimize a slow query - what would you check?
questions.header: Stage 7 - SQL / MySQL: Extra interview Q&A (with queries)

blocks.text: 1) Run EXPLAIN/EXPLAIN ANALYZE - look for 'Using filesort', 'Using temporary', large 'rows' estimates, sequential scans. 2) Check indexes on columns in WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY. 3) Composite indexes - column order matters (leftmost prefix rule). 4) Avoid SELECT * - fetch only needed columns. 5) Replace correlated subqueries with JOINs. 6) Limit row count early in pipeline. 7) Check for implicit type conversion (string vs int in WHERE - kills index). 8) Pagination: use cursor-based (WHERE id > last) instead of LIMIT/OFFSET for large offsets. 9) Check DB stats are up to date (ANALYZE TABLE). 10) Look at locks - LOCK WAIT timeouts in logs.

questions.qText: What is a covering index? When useful?
questions.header: Stage 7 - SQL / MySQL: Extra interview Q&A (with queries)

blocks.text: Covering index includes ALL columns the query needs (in WHERE, SELECT, ORDER BY). The database can serve the query entirely from the index without reading the table - much faster. Example: 'SELECT name, email FROM users WHERE status=1' - if you create INDEX(status, name, email), the index alone satisfies the query. EXPLAIN shows 'Using index'. Trade-off: larger index, slower writes.

questions.qText: Explain composite index and leftmost prefix rule.
questions.header: Stage 7 - SQL / MySQL: Extra interview Q&A (with queries)

blocks.text: Composite index = index on multiple columns. Order matters! Index on (a, b, c) helps queries on: a, a+b, a+b+c, a+c (partial), but NOT b alone, c alone, b+c. The 'leftmost prefix' rule: the index can be used if the WHERE clause uses columns from the left of the index definition contiguously. So for WHERE a=1 AND b=2 - good. For WHERE b=2 - index not used. Design composite indexes based on actual query patterns.

questions.qText: How does an INDEX work internally (B-tree)?
questions.header: Stage 7 - SQL / MySQL: Extra interview Q&A (with queries)

blocks.text: Most DB indexes use B+ trees. A B+ tree is a balanced m-ary tree where internal nodes hold only keys (for navigation) and all data lives in leaf nodes. Leaves are linked for efficient range scans. Height is logarithmic - for billions of rows, height is just 3-5. Each search compares O(log n) keys. Insertions/deletions rebalance the tree. Why not hash indexes? B-trees support range queries, ORDER BY, and prefix matches; hash only equality. Modern DBs do also offer hash indexes (PostgreSQL), GIN, BRIN, etc. for specific needs.

questions.qText: Difference between CHAR, VARCHAR, and TEXT in MySQL?
questions.header: Stage 7 - SQL / MySQL: Extra interview Q&A (with queries)

blocks.text: CHAR(n): fixed length, padded with spaces. Fast, but wastes space for short values. Use for short fixed strings like country codes (CHAR(2)). VARCHAR(n): variable length up to n. Uses 1-2 bytes for length prefix. Use for most string columns up to 65,535 bytes. TEXT: variable length large strings, stored separately from row. Use for long content like blog posts, descriptions. Indexing TEXT requires specifying prefix length. Choose based on length and access patterns.

questions.qText: What is ACID and how does MySQL implement it?
questions.header: Stage 7 - SQL / MySQL: Extra interview Q&A (with queries)

blocks.text: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability - properties guaranteeing reliable transactions.

MySQL (InnoDB engine) implements: A - via undo logs (rollback on failure). C - via constraints, foreign keys. I - via MVCC + locking (different isolation levels). D - via redo logs (write-ahead log flushed to disk on commit; double write buffer for crash safety).
MyISAM (older engine) doesn't support transactions - no ACID. Always use InnoDB for production.

questions.qText: What are the four transaction isolation levels with anomalies?
questions.header: Stage 7 - SQL / MySQL: Extra interview Q&A (with queries)

blocks.text: READ UNCOMMITTED: lowest. Allows DIRTY READ - seeing uncommitted changes from other transactions.
READ COMMITTED: only sees committed data. Prevents dirty read. Still allows NON-REPEATABLE READ - reading same row twice gives different values.
REPEATABLE READ (MySQL default): same row read twice in a transaction returns same value. Allows PHANTOM READ - new matching rows can appear in a range query.
SERIALIZABLE: highest. Full isolation as if transactions ran one at a time. Most locking overhead.

lines: SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;
SELECT * FROM accounts WHERE id = 1;  -- repeatable reads guaranteed

questions.qText: Explain SELECT FOR UPDATE.
questions.header: Stage 7 - SQL / MySQL: Extra interview Q&A (with queries)

blocks.text: SELECT FOR UPDATE acquires an EXCLUSIVE row-level lock on the selected rows. Other transactions trying to read with FOR UPDATE or write those rows will WAIT. Used for pessimistic locking - e.g., reading account balance to deduct money. Without it, two transactions might both read $100, both decide to deduct $30, and end with $70 instead of $40. Pair with @Transactional in Spring.

questions.qText: When would you denormalize a database?
questions.header: Stage 7 - SQL / MySQL: Extra interview Q&A (with queries)

blocks.text: Denormalize when: 1) Read performance is critical and joins are expensive. 2) Data is mostly read, rarely updated. 3) You can tolerate some inconsistency or have eventual sync. Common patterns: store user_name in orders to avoid joining users table on every order display; precomputed counts (like_count on posts) updated via triggers or async jobs; materialized views. Trade-off: faster reads, more complex writes, risk of inconsistency. Start normalized; denormalize when measurements show a real bottleneck.

questions.qText: Difference between TRUNCATE, DELETE, and DROP.
questions.header: Stage 7 - SQL / MySQL: Extra interview Q&A (with queries)

blocks.text: DELETE: DML. Removes rows matching WHERE (or all if no WHERE). Logged row by row. Can be rolled back. Fires triggers. Keeps the table structure and auto-increment counter. TRUNCATE: DDL. Removes ALL rows quickly by deallocating data pages. Cannot have WHERE. Implicit commit. Resets auto-increment. Doesn't fire row triggers. Much faster than DELETE for full table wipe. DROP: DDL. Removes the entire table - structure, data, indexes, triggers, permissions. Implicit commit. Use case: DELETE for selective row removal, TRUNCATE for clearing a table, DROP for removing it entirely.

questions.qText: What is MongoDB and how is it different from a SQL database?
questions.header: Stage 7 - MongoDB: Extra interview Q&A (with commands)

blocks.text: MongoDB is a document-oriented NoSQL database. Data is stored as flexible BSON (Binary JSON) documents inside collections. No fixed schema - different documents in the same collection can have different fields. Horizontal scaling via sharding. Indexes work similarly to SQL. Trade-off: no joins (use embedding or $lookup), no multi-document ACID without explicit transactions (since 4.0 it does). Best for: hierarchical data, evolving schemas, high write throughput, catalogs, content management.

questions.qText: Embedding vs Referencing - when to use which?
questions.header: Stage 7 - MongoDB: Extra interview Q&A (with commands)

blocks.text: Embed (nested document) when:
- The child data is always read with the parent (one-to-few).
- Data doesn't change often.
- Total document stays under 16MB.
Example: order with line items embedded - you almost always need them together.
Reference (store ID, look up separately) when:
- Many-to-many or unbounded one-to-many.
- Child is independently queried or updated.
- Data is shared across many parents.
Example: user references posts they wrote (posts queried separately too).

questions.qText: Sharding in MongoDB - how does it work?
questions.header: Stage 7 - MongoDB: Extra interview Q&A (with commands)

blocks.text: Sharding distributes data across multiple machines for horizontal scaling. Components: 1) Shards - data partitions, each is usually a replica set. 2) Config servers - store metadata about chunk ranges. 3) Mongos routers - clients connect to mongos, which routes queries based on shard key. Shard key choice is critical - it determines distribution. Good keys: high cardinality, even write distribution, often used in queries. Bad: monotonically increasing (creates hotspot on one shard) or low cardinality. Hash sharding distributes evenly but loses range query efficiency.

questions.qText: Replica set in MongoDB - how does failover work?
questions.header: Stage 7 - MongoDB: Extra interview Q&A (with commands)

blocks.text: Replica set: group of MongoDB instances replicating same data. Typical: 1 primary + 2+ secondaries. Writes go to primary; replicated to secondaries via oplog (operations log). Reads default to primary, can be set to secondary (with read concern). Failover: if primary unreachable, secondaries trigger election. Need majority of voting members for new primary. New primary elected within ~10 seconds. Apps with proper driver retry policies see brief disruption. Use writeConcern: 'majority' to ensure write durability across replicas.

questions.qText: What is a capped collection?

blocks.text: Capped collection has a fixed size (and optionally max doc count). Documents inserted in order. Once full, oldest documents are automatically overwritten - circular buffer behavior. Insertion order preserved on disk. Used for: logs, real-time data, audit trails where only recent data matters. Created via 'db.createCollection("logs", { capped: true, size: 100000000, max: 1000000 })'. Cannot delete docs from capped collection; only overwrite by new inserts.

blocks.text: String: simple key-value. SET, GET, INCR. Caching, counters, session data.
List: ordered, allow duplicates. LPUSH/RPUSH/LPOP/RPOP. Use as queues, recent items list.
Set: unique unordered. SADD, SREM, SMEMBERS, SINTER (intersection). Tags, unique visitors, social: A's friends ∩ B's friends.

questions.qText: Redis persistence - RDB vs AOF?
questions.header: Stage 7 - Redis: Extra interview Q&A (with commands)

blocks.text: RDB (Redis Database file): point-in-time snapshots. Fast to load, compact. Configured: 'save 900 1' (save if 1 change in 900s). Risk: lose data since last snapshot. Good for backups.
AOF (Append Only File): logs every write operation. Replay on startup to rebuild state. Configurable fsync: always (slow, safe), everysec (default, good balance), no (OS flushes when it wants).
Best practice: use BOTH. RDB for fast restart + AOF for durability. Set appendfsync everysec - at most 1 second of data loss.

questions.qText: Eviction policies in Redis.
questions.header: Stage 7 - Redis: Extra interview Q&A (with commands)

blocks.text: When memory hits maxmemory limit, Redis decides what to evict. Policies: noeviction (errors on writes - default, bad for cache). allkeys-lru (evict least recently used from any key). allkeys-lfu (least frequently used - better for cache hit ratio). volatile-lru / volatile-lfu (only from keys with TTL set). volatile-ttl (closest to expiry first). allkeys-random / volatile-random. For pure caching, use allkeys-lru or allkeys-lfu. Set in redis.conf: 'maxmemory 2gb' and 'maxmemory-policy allkeys-lfu'.

questions.qText: Difference between SQL and NoSQL?

blocks.text: SQL (relational): structured data in tables with predefined schema, uses SQL language, ACID guarantees, vertical scaling primarily, joins are natural. Examples: MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server.

NoSQL: flexible schema, multiple types (document, key-value, columnar, graph), horizontal scaling, eventual consistency often, no joins (denormalize). Examples: MongoDB (document), Redis (key-value), Cassandra (columnar), Neo4j (graph).
When to use which: SQL for transactional systems (banking, ERP, anything with relationships). NoSQL for high-velocity, semi-structured data (logs, user activity, catalogs). Most modern apps use BOTH.

questions.qText: Explain ACID properties.

blocks.text: Atomicity: a transaction is all-or-nothing. If any step fails, all changes roll back.
Consistency: a transaction takes the DB from one valid state to another, respecting all constraints (foreign keys, unique, check).
Isolation: concurrent transactions don't interfere; the result is as if they ran serially. Different isolation levels trade off correctness vs performance.
Durability: once committed, changes survive crashes (written to disk, replicated, etc.).

questions.qText: Difference between INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, and FULL JOIN.

blocks.text: INNER JOIN: returns only rows where the join condition matches in both tables.
LEFT JOIN: returns all rows from left table, plus matching rows from right (NULL if no match). Used when 'I want all customers, even if they have no orders'.
RIGHT JOIN: mirror of LEFT - all from right, matching from left. Rarely used (just swap tables and use LEFT).
FULL OUTER JOIN: all rows from both tables, NULLs where no match. MySQL doesn't support directly; emulate with LEFT UNION RIGHT.

questions.qText: Difference between WHERE and HAVING?

blocks.text: WHERE filters individual rows BEFORE grouping. HAVING filters groups AFTER aggregation. You can use aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG) in HAVING but not in WHERE. Example: 'SELECT dept, COUNT(*) FROM employees WHERE salary > 50000 GROUP BY dept HAVING COUNT(*) > 5' - filters employees by salary first, then groups by department, then keeps only departments with more than 5 such employees.

lines: SELECT DISTINCT salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 1 OFFSET N-1;

lines: SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees WHERE salary NOT IN (
SELECT DISTINCT TOP N-1 salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC

questions.qText: What are normalization and its forms?

blocks.text: Normalization is organizing data to reduce redundancy and improve integrity.
1NF: atomic values (no arrays/lists in cells), unique rows, no repeating groups.
2NF: 1NF + every non-key attribute fully depends on the WHOLE primary key (no partial dependency).
3NF: 2NF + no transitive dependency (non-key attributes don't depend on other non-key attributes).
BCNF: stricter 3NF - every determinant is a candidate key.
Most production tables aim for 3NF or BCNF. Denormalize selectively for read performance.

questions.qText: What is an index? When to use, when to avoid?

blocks.text: An index is a separate data structure (usually B-tree) that speeds up lookups on a column. Trade-off: faster reads, slower writes (index must be updated).
Use index on: columns frequently in WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY; columns with high cardinality (many unique values).
Avoid index on: small tables, columns with low cardinality (gender), columns rarely queried, write-heavy tables with many indexes.
Composite index: order matters. Index on (a, b) helps queries on 'a' or 'a and b', but not on 'b' alone.

questions.qText: Clustered vs non-clustered index?

blocks.text: Clustered index: defines the physical order of rows in the table. One per table (typically on primary key). Lookups are very fast. Non-clustered index: separate structure with pointers to rows. Many per table. Slightly slower than clustered because of the extra hop. In MySQL InnoDB, the PRIMARY KEY is automatically clustered. Secondary indexes point to the primary key value, not directly to disk location.

questions.qText: Difference between DELETE, TRUNCATE, and DROP?

blocks.text: DELETE: DML, removes rows based on WHERE, can be rolled back, fires triggers, slower (logs each row), keeps auto-increment counter.
TRUNCATE: DDL, removes ALL rows instantly, cannot be rolled back in MySQL (in some DBs it can within transaction), doesn't fire row triggers, resets auto-increment, faster (deallocates pages).
DROP: DDL, removes the entire table (structure + data + indexes + permissions). Cannot be rolled back.

questions.qText: What is a stored procedure?

blocks.text: A stored procedure is a precompiled SQL routine stored in the database, called by name with parameters. Benefits: encapsulates business logic in DB, reduces network traffic, can be more performant, security (grant execute without table access). Drawbacks: harder to version control, DB-vendor specific, harder to test. Modern apps usually keep logic in application code, but some enterprises rely heavily on stored procs.

questions.qText: Explain transaction isolation levels.

blocks.text: READ UNCOMMITTED: lowest, allows dirty reads (seeing uncommitted changes). Rarely used.
READ COMMITTED: only sees committed data; no dirty reads. But same query in same transaction may return different results (non-repeatable read). Default in PostgreSQL, Oracle.
REPEATABLE READ: same row read twice returns same result. But new rows can appear (phantom reads). Default in MySQL InnoDB.
SERIALIZABLE: highest, fully isolated; transactions appear to run sequentially. Most expensive due to extensive locking.

questions.qText: What is a deadlock? How to handle?

blocks.text: Deadlock: two transactions are waiting for each other's locks, neither can proceed. Example: T1 locks row A, wants row B; T2 locks row B, wants row A. Detection: DB engine detects cycle, kills one transaction (victim) and rolls it back. Prevention: 1) Access tables/rows in consistent order. 2) Keep transactions short. 3) Use lower isolation levels when safe. 4) Retry deadlocked transactions in app code (catch the specific exception).

questions.qText: What is a CTE (Common Table Expression)?

blocks.text: A CTE is a named temporary result set defined with WITH clause, usable within a single statement. Improves readability for complex queries with subqueries.

lines: FROM employees e JOIN dept_avg d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id

blocks.text: Recursive CTEs handle hierarchical data (org chart, file system tree).

questions.qText: What are window functions? Give an example.

blocks.text: Window functions perform calculations across a 'window' of rows related to current row, without collapsing them like GROUP BY does.

lines: RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rank_in_dept

blocks.text: Common functions: ROW_NUMBER (unique sequential), RANK (gaps on ties), DENSE_RANK (no gaps), LAG/LEAD (previous/next row), SUM/AVG/MIN/MAX OVER. Used for top-N per group, running totals, period-over-period comparisons.

questions.qText: Find duplicate emails in a users table.
What is sharding? Vertical vs horizontal partitioning?

blocks.text: Sharding: splitting a database horizontally across multiple servers (each shard has subset of rows).
Vertical partitioning: splitting a table by COLUMNS (rarely accessed columns in another table).
Horizontal partitioning: splitting by ROWS (range, hash, list). Sharding is horizontal partitioning across SEPARATE servers.
Sharding strategies: range-based (user_id 1-1M on shard1, 1M-2M on shard2), hash-based (hash(user_id) % N), geographic. Trade-off: harder cross-shard queries, complex rebalancing.

questions.qText: MongoDB vs MySQL - when to use which?

blocks.text: MySQL (and other RDBMS): when data has clear relationships, you need ACID transactions across multiple tables, complex queries with joins. Examples: banking, e-commerce orders, ERP. MongoDB: flexible/evolving schema, hierarchical data that's always read together, high write volume, horizontal scaling needed. Examples: product catalogs, user profiles, content management, real-time analytics. Many modern apps use both: MySQL for transactions, MongoDB for activity logs.

questions.qText: How does Redis achieve high performance?

blocks.text: 1) In-memory: all data in RAM (vs disk). Microsecond latencies. 2) Single-threaded event loop: no thread context switches, no locks for data operations. 3) Optimized data structures (hash tables, skip lists for sorted sets). 4) Pipelining: send multiple commands without waiting for replies. 5) Connection multiplexing. Caveat: long-running commands block everything else, so avoid SELECT * style operations. Use SCAN instead of KEYS for large datasets.

questions.qText: Difference between cache-aside and write-through caching?

blocks.text: Cache-aside (lazy loading): app checks cache first. On miss, fetches from DB, writes to cache, returns. Most common pattern. Pros: only caches what's used. Cons: stale data on writes (must invalidate).
Write-through: app writes to cache, cache writes synchronously to DB. Pros: cache always fresh. Cons: write latency, caches data that may not be read.
Write-behind (write-back): app writes to cache, cache writes to DB asynchronously. Fast writes but risk of data loss on crash.

questions.qText: What is a primary key vs unique key vs foreign key?

blocks.text: Primary key: uniquely identifies each row, NOT NULL, only one per table, automatically indexed (often clustered). Unique key: ensures uniqueness in a column/columns, allows one NULL (in most DBs), can have multiple per table. Foreign key: references primary key of another table, enforces referential integrity (can't reference non-existent row, can't delete parent if children exist - unless ON DELETE CASCADE).

questions.qText: Optimistic vs pessimistic locking?

blocks.text: Optimistic: assumes conflicts are rare. Reads don't lock. Before update, check if data was modified (via version column or timestamp). If yes, abort/retry. Better for read-heavy systems.
Pessimistic: assumes conflicts are likely. Locks the row when read (SELECT FOR UPDATE), blocks other transactions until done. Better for write-heavy contention.
JPA @Version annotation implements optimistic locking. SELECT FOR UPDATE implements pessimistic.

questions.qText: Explain MongoDB aggregation pipeline.

blocks.text: Aggregation pipeline processes documents through a sequence of stages, each transforming output of the previous. Stages: $match (filter), $group (aggregate), $project (select/compute fields), $sort, $limit, $skip, $unwind (expand arrays), $lookup (join with another collection), $addFields, $facet (multi-pipeline). Example: $match pending orders → $group by customer → $sort by total desc → $limit 10 = top 10 customers by pending order value.

questions.qText: Kafka concepts: explain offsets, partitions, consumer groups.
questions.header: Stage 8 - Spring Boot: Extra scenario & coding Q&A

blocks.text: Partition: a topic is split into partitions for parallelism. Each partition is an ordered, immutable sequence. Order is guaranteed WITHIN a partition, not across.
Offset: each message in a partition gets a sequential number (0, 1, 2, ...). Consumer tracks 'last read offset' per partition.

Consumer group: set of consumers sharing the work for a topic. Kafka assigns each partition to exactly ONE consumer in the group. If group has 3 consumers and topic has 6 partitions, each consumer gets 2 partitions. If 10 consumers and 6 partitions, 4 sit idle.
Multiple groups can read the same topic independently - one group for payment-service, another for analytics-service.
Rebalancing: when a consumer joins/leaves, Kafka redistributes partitions. During rebalance, no consumption happens (small pause).

questions.qText: Difference between Kafka and RabbitMQ - when to use which?
questions.header: Stage 8 - Spring Boot: Extra scenario & coding Q&A

blocks.text: Kafka: distributed commit log. High throughput (millions msg/sec), messages retained for hours/days (replayable), partition-based parallelism, consumer pulls. Strengths: event streaming, log aggregation, replay, multiple independent consumer groups on same data.

RabbitMQ: traditional message broker. Lower throughput (tens of thousands/sec), rich routing (exchanges, bindings), priority queues, request-reply, smaller messages. Strengths: complex routing, low-latency, RPC-style messaging, work queues.
Quick rule: streaming/analytics/event-sourcing -> Kafka. Task queues/RPC/complex routing -> RabbitMQ.

questions.qText: Spring Bean lifecycle - explain in detail.
questions.header: Stage 8 - Spring Boot: Extra scenario & coding Q&A

blocks.text: Spring bean lifecycle stages:
1) Instantiation - Spring creates the bean (calls constructor).
2) Populate properties - Spring injects dependencies.
3) Aware interfaces - if bean implements BeanNameAware, BeanFactoryAware, ApplicationContextAware, those callbacks fire.
4) BeanPostProcessor.postProcessBeforeInitialization() - all registered post-processors run.
5) Init - @PostConstruct method, then InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet(), then custom init-method.
6) BeanPostProcessor.postProcessAfterInitialization().
7) Bean is ready for use.
8) On shutdown: @PreDestroy method, then DisposableBean.destroy(), then custom destroy-method.

questions.qText: What is Spring Boot and how does it differ from Spring?

blocks.text: Spring Framework provides the core IoC container, DI, MVC, AOP - but configuring it is verbose (lots of XML or Java config).

Spring Boot is an opinionated layer over Spring that adds: 1) Auto-configuration - looks at classpath and configures beans automatically. 2) Starter dependencies - one dependency pulls in everything you need (e.g., spring-boot-starter-web). 3) Embedded server - runs as a JAR, no external Tomcat. 4) Production-ready features via Actuator. 5) Spring Initializr for project generation.
Result: 'mvn spring-boot:run' and you have a running REST API in minutes, not hours.

questions.qText: What is Inversion of Control and Dependency Injection?

blocks.text: IoC: the framework controls the flow, not your code. Instead of you creating objects and managing their lifecycle, Spring does it.

DI: a specific form of IoC. Instead of a class creating its dependencies (new UserRepository()), the dependencies are injected (constructor, setter, or field). The class declares 'I need a UserRepository' and Spring provides one.
Benefits: loose coupling (depends on interface, not concrete class), testability (inject mocks), reusability.

questions.qText: What are the types of dependency injection in Spring? Which is best?

blocks.text: 1) Constructor injection: dependencies passed via constructor. RECOMMENDED. Pros: dependencies are required and immutable (final), fails fast if missing, easy to test, no Spring needed to construct.
2) Setter injection: via setter methods. Useful for optional dependencies. Cons: mutable, can be in inconsistent state.
3) Field injection: @Autowired on field directly. Looks clean but bad - can't be final, hard to test without reflection, hides dependencies. Avoid.
Modern Spring favors constructor injection - Lombok's @RequiredArgsConstructor makes it concise.

questions.qText: Explain Spring bean scopes.

blocks.text: singleton (default): one instance per Spring container. Same object for every injection.
prototype: new instance every time it's requested.
request (web): one per HTTP request.
session (web): one per HTTP session.
application (web): one per ServletContext.
websocket: one per WebSocket session.
Use singleton for stateless services, prototype for stateful objects.

questions.qText: What does @SpringBootApplication do?

blocks.text: It's a meta-annotation combining 3 things: 1) @Configuration - marks the class as a source of bean definitions. 2) @EnableAutoConfiguration - tells Spring Boot to auto-configure based on classpath. 3) @ComponentScan - scans this package and sub-packages for @Component, @Service, @Repository, @Controller. Effectively the single annotation that bootstraps a Spring Boot app.

questions.qText: How does auto-configuration work?

blocks.text: On startup, Spring Boot reads META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports (Spring Boot 3) or spring.factories (Spring Boot 2) from all JARs on the classpath. These files list auto-configuration classes.

Each auto-config class has @Conditional annotations: @ConditionalOnClass (only if a class is on classpath), @ConditionalOnMissingBean (only if bean doesn't already exist), @ConditionalOnProperty, etc.

Example: spring-boot-starter-data-jpa pulls in DataSourceAutoConfiguration. It activates only if DataSource class is on classpath and you've configured DB properties. Then it creates a DataSource bean, EntityManagerFactory, transaction manager - all from defaults.

questions.qText: Difference between @Component, @Service, @Repository, @Controller?

blocks.text: All four are stereotypes - Spring treats them the same for component scanning. The semantic differences: @Component - generic. @Service - business logic layer. @Repository - data access layer; ALSO enables exception translation (SQLException -> Spring's DataAccessException). @Controller - web layer, returns view names. @RestController = @Controller + @ResponseBody, returns body directly (for REST APIs). Use the most specific one for clarity and to benefit from @Repository's exception translation.

questions.qText: How do you handle exceptions globally in Spring Boot?

blocks.text: Use @ControllerAdvice with @ExceptionHandler methods.

Best practice: have a hierarchy of custom exceptions (BusinessException, ResourceNotFoundException, ValidationException), a standard error response DTO, and handlers for each. Spring's ResponseEntityExceptionHandler is a good base class - extend it.

questions.qText: What is the difference between @RequestParam and @PathVariable?

blocks.text: @PathVariable extracts values from URL path - /users/{id}, the {id} is a path variable. @RequestParam extracts values from query string - /users?name=John&age=25. PathVariable is for resource identifiers (RESTful); RequestParam is for filters, sorting, pagination.

questions.qText: Explain @Transactional. Default isolation and propagation?

blocks.text: @Transactional makes a method execute within a database transaction. Spring wraps the method with begin/commit/rollback logic. Rollback happens on RuntimeException by default - NOT on checked exceptions (configure rollbackFor).
Default isolation: DEFAULT (uses DB's default - usually READ_COMMITTED in PostgreSQL, REPEATABLE_READ in MySQL).

Default propagation: REQUIRED - join existing transaction or create new one. Other modes: REQUIRES_NEW (suspend outer, create new), NESTED (savepoint within outer), MANDATORY (must have outer), SUPPORTS (use if exists), NOT_SUPPORTED (suspend), NEVER (fail if exists).
Pitfall: @Transactional only works on PUBLIC methods called from OUTSIDE the class (proxy-based AOP). Calling a @Transactional method from another method in the same class bypasses the proxy.

questions.qText: What is the N+1 problem in JPA? How to fix it?

blocks.text: N+1 problem: fetching a list of N parent entities triggers N additional queries to load their children (because relationships are lazy-loaded). For 100 orders with line items, you do 1 + 100 = 101 queries.

Fixes: 1) JOIN FETCH in JPQL: 'SELECT o FROM Order o JOIN FETCH o.items'. Loads all in one query. 2) @EntityGraph annotation. 3) Use projections/DTOs - only fetch what you need. 4) @BatchSize annotation to fetch in chunks. 5) Avoid LAZY loading for relationships always read together (carefully).
Detect: log SQL queries in dev (spring.jpa.show-sql=true). If you see a query loop, you have N+1.

questions.qText: Difference between save() and saveAndFlush()?

blocks.text: save() persists the entity but the actual SQL may be deferred to end of transaction or flush time. The entity is in the persistence context. saveAndFlush() persists AND immediately flushes to DB - SQL executes right away. Use saveAndFlush when you need the DB-generated ID immediately, or want to commit changes for visibility in same transaction. Otherwise save() is fine; the transaction's commit will flush at the end.

questions.qText: What is the difference between JPA and Hibernate?

blocks.text: JPA (Java Persistence API): a specification - defines interfaces and annotations (EntityManager, @Entity, @Id, etc.). Hibernate: an implementation of JPA, plus its own features (Hibernate-specific APIs, custom annotations). Spring Data JPA: a convenience layer on top of JPA - gives you repositories with derived queries, removing boilerplate. Stack: Spring Data JPA → JPA spec → Hibernate (default) → JDBC → DB.

questions.qText: How do you secure REST APIs with JWT in Spring Boot?

blocks.text: Flow: 1) User logs in with credentials. 2) Server validates, generates JWT (signed with secret), returns to client. 3) Client sends JWT in Authorization header on every request: 'Bearer <token>'. 4) Server validates JWT signature and extracts user info.

Implementation: 1) Add jjwt or nimbus-jose-jwt dependency. 2) Create a JwtAuthenticationFilter (extends OncePerRequestFilter) that reads token, validates, sets SecurityContext. 3) Configure SecurityFilterChain to: disable CSRF (stateless), use SessionCreationPolicy.STATELESS, add the filter before UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter, permitAll() on /auth/login, authenticated() elsewhere.
For refresh tokens: store securely (httpOnly cookie or DB), shorter access token TTL (15 min), longer refresh TTL (7 days), rotate refresh on use.

questions.qText: What is CORS? How to configure in Spring Boot?

blocks.text: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing): browser security restriction. A page on https://app.com cannot make AJAX calls to https://api.com unless the server allows it via CORS headers.
Configure in Spring Boot: 1) @CrossOrigin annotation on controller (per-endpoint). 2) Global config via WebMvcConfigurer:
With Spring Security, also configure CORS in SecurityFilterChain BEFORE other filters.

questions.qText: Explain Spring Boot Actuator. Common endpoints?

blocks.text: Spring Boot Actuator exposes operational endpoints for monitoring and managing the app. Common endpoints: /actuator/health (UP/DOWN with component details), /actuator/info (custom info), /actuator/metrics (JVM, HTTP metrics), /actuator/env (environment), /actuator/beans (all beans), /actuator/mappings (request mappings), /actuator/loggers (change log levels at runtime), /actuator/threaddump, /actuator/heapdump. In production, secure them - typically only health and info are public; others require authentication.

questions.qText: What are the different ways to make a method asynchronous in Spring Boot?

blocks.text: 1) @Async annotation - simplest. Add @EnableAsync to config and @Async on the method. Returns void, Future, or CompletableFuture. Spring runs it on a TaskExecutor. 2) CompletableFuture.supplyAsync() - manually compose async pipelines. 3) ExecutorService directly - low-level. 4) Reactive (Mono/Flux) with Spring WebFlux - different paradigm. 5) Virtual threads (Java 21+) - native lightweight threads. @Async pitfall: like @Transactional, only works on calls from OUTSIDE the class.

questions.qText: What is the difference between @Async and parallelStream?

blocks.text: @Async: makes a method run on a different thread, called once. Caller continues immediately. Returns Future/CompletableFuture. Use for: fire-and-forget tasks (email, logging), parallelizing independent operations. parallelStream: splits a stream's processing across ForkJoinPool threads. Same method call, internal parallelism. Use for: CPU-bound batch processing on collections where order doesn't matter. Watch out: parallelStream uses common ForkJoinPool which is shared - one slow operation can block others.

questions.qText: How do you test a Spring Boot REST controller?

blocks.text: Use @WebMvcTest for slice testing (loads only the web layer):
For full integration test: @SpringBootTest with TestRestTemplate or WebTestClient. For DB: TestContainers spins up a real DB in Docker.

questions.qText: What is Spring Profiles? How to use it?

blocks.text: Profiles let you have different configurations per environment (dev, test, staging, prod). Define application-dev.yml, application-prod.yml. Activate via: spring.profiles.active=prod (in application.yml, env var SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE, or --spring.profiles.active=prod cmd line). Use @Profile("dev") on beans or @Profile("!prod") for negation. Common pattern: a DevDataLoader @Profile("dev") that seeds test data only in dev.

questions.qText: How do you handle database migrations in Spring Boot?

blocks.text: Flyway or Liquibase. Add the dependency, Spring Boot auto-configures them. Flyway: place SQL files in src/main/resources/db/migration with names like V1__create_users.sql, V2__add_email_column.sql. On startup, Flyway runs new migrations in order and tracks applied ones in flyway_schema_history table. Liquibase: uses XML/YAML/JSON changelogs - more powerful for cross-DB compatibility but verbose. Rule: NEVER use ddl-auto=update in production - use migrations.

questions.qText: Explain how @Cacheable works.

blocks.text: With @EnableCaching, Spring uses AOP to wrap methods. @Cacheable on a method: 1) Before invocation, computes cache key (default: method args). 2) Looks up in cache. 3) If hit, returns cached value, skips method. 4) If miss, invokes method, stores result in cache, returns it. @CachePut: always invokes method, updates cache. @CacheEvict: removes entry. Pitfall: same as @Transactional - proxy-based, doesn't work on self-invocation. Use Redis as cache provider for production (distributed cache).

questions.qText: Spring Cloud - what is it and what are its components?

blocks.text: Spring Cloud is a collection of libraries for building distributed systems / microservices in Spring.

Key components: Spring Cloud Gateway (routing, filters, rate limiting). Spring Cloud Config (centralized configuration). Eureka or Consul (service discovery). OpenFeign (declarative REST client). Resilience4j (circuit breakers, retries). Sleuth + Zipkin / Micrometer Tracing (distributed tracing). Spring Cloud Stream (messaging abstraction over Kafka/RabbitMQ). Spring Cloud Bus (refresh configs across services).

questions.qText: What is a circuit breaker? How does Resilience4j implement it?

blocks.text: Circuit breaker prevents cascading failures. If a downstream service is failing, the breaker 'opens' and short-circuits calls - fails fast instead of waiting for timeouts.
States: CLOSED (normal - calls pass through), OPEN (calls fail immediately for a duration), HALF_OPEN (test calls allowed; success closes, failure re-opens).

Resilience4j: add @CircuitBreaker(name="backend", fallbackMethod="fallback"). Configure in YAML: failureRateThreshold (50%), slowCallDurationThreshold, waitDurationInOpenState, slidingWindowSize. The fallback method is called when breaker is open.

Consumer:
Configure bootstrap-servers, serializers (JsonSerializer), and groupId in application.yml. For error handling, configure DefaultErrorHandler with retry + dead-letter topic.

questions.qText: How to choose between Kafka and RabbitMQ?

blocks.text: Kafka: high throughput, log/event streaming, replayability (consumers can re-read by changing offset), partitioning, multiple consumer groups can independently consume the same topic. Better for: event sourcing, analytics pipelines, audit logs, high-volume data ingestion. RabbitMQ: traditional message broker, complex routing (exchanges, bindings), per-message acknowledgment, priority queues, lower latency for small messages. Better for: task queues, RPC-style messaging, complex routing logic, smaller scale.

questions.qText: What is the Saga pattern?

blocks.text: Saga handles distributed transactions across microservices when ACID isn't possible. Break the transaction into local transactions, each with a compensating action to undo it.

Two styles: Choreography - each service publishes events; others react. No central coordinator. Simpler for few steps but hard to track. Orchestration - a central orchestrator tells each service what to do next, and what to undo on failure. Easier to debug, more complex code.
Example: order placement = reserve inventory (Inventory service) + charge card (Payment service) + create shipment (Shipping service). If Payment fails, compensating actions: release inventory.

questions.qText: What is the difference between RestTemplate and WebClient?

blocks.text: RestTemplate: synchronous, blocking HTTP client (Spring's old standard). Each call blocks the calling thread until response. Simple but doesn't scale for high I/O. In maintenance mode since Spring 5. WebClient: non-blocking, reactive (built on Reactor). Same thread can handle many in-flight requests. Use for high concurrency, calling many services in parallel, or in reactive stacks (WebFlux). For most cases today, use WebClient (synchronously via .block() if you don't need full reactivity).

questions.qText: How do you implement idempotency in REST APIs?

blocks.text: Idempotency: same request produces same result if repeated. GET/PUT/DELETE are naturally idempotent. POST usually isn't. Pattern: client generates Idempotency-Key (UUID) and sends in header. Server: 1) Check if key exists in storage (Redis or DB). 2) If yes, return cached response. 3) If no, process request, store (key, response). 4) Set TTL (24 hours typical). Now retrying the same request returns the same result, no duplicate charge or order.

questions.qText: Spring Boot startup is slow - how to debug?

blocks.text: 1) Enable debug logging: --debug shows auto-configuration report. 2) Look at /actuator/startup endpoint - shows time per phase. 3) Use Spring Context Indexer (META-INF/spring.components) to skip classpath scanning. 4) Use lazy initialization: spring.main.lazy-initialization=true (beans created on first use). 5) Disable unused auto-configurations: spring.autoconfigure.exclude. 6) GraalVM Native Image for instant startup (advanced). Common culprits: too many beans, expensive @PostConstruct, JPA scanning many entities, slow DB connection.

questions.qText: Service discovery in microservices - approaches.
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps + Microservices: Scenario Q&A with YAML/configs

blocks.text: Client-side discovery: each service queries the registry, picks an instance, calls directly. Examples: Eureka (Netflix) + Ribbon. Pro: less hops. Con: every client needs discovery logic.

Server-side discovery: client calls a load balancer / API gateway, which queries the registry. Examples: Kubernetes services (kube-proxy uses iptables / IPVS), AWS ALB, Consul + Envoy. Pro: clients are simpler. Con: extra hop.

In Kubernetes, you typically don't need a separate registry - K8s handles it. Each Service gets a DNS name. CoreDNS resolves <service>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local. kube-proxy load-balances to healthy pods. Service mesh (Istio, Linkerd) adds advanced features.

questions.qText: API Gateway - what is it and why use one?
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps + Microservices: Scenario Q&A with YAML/configs

blocks.text: Single entry point for all client requests to your microservices. Responsibilities: request routing, authentication/authorization, rate limiting, request/response transformation, caching, CORS, logging, metrics. Benefits: clients don't need to know all services, central security enforcement, can aggregate calls. Options: Spring Cloud Gateway (Java), Kong, AWS API Gateway, NGINX Plus, Envoy.

questions.qText: Centralized logging - the ELK / Loki approach.
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps + Microservices: Scenario Q&A with YAML/configs

blocks.text: Each app writes structured logs (JSON) to stdout. K8s captures pod stdout to a node-level log file. A log collector (Fluentd, Fluent Bit, Promtail) tails these files and ships to a central store. Store + search: Elasticsearch + Kibana (ELK) or Grafana Loki (cheaper, label-based). Each log entry includes service name, environment, version, traceId. Engineers search across all services from one UI.

questions.qText: Twelve-factor app principles - explain with examples.
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps + Microservices: Scenario Q&A with YAML/configs

blocks.text: 12-factor methodology for cloud-native apps:
1) Codebase - one codebase tracked in version control, many deploys.
2) Dependencies - explicitly declare (pom.xml/package.json), no system-wide assumptions.
3) Config - in environment variables, never in code. Different per env.
4) Backing services - DBs, caches, queues are attached resources (URL in env).
5) Build/release/run - strictly separate stages. Build creates artifact, release combines with config, run executes.
6) Processes - stateless. State in DB/cache.
7) Port binding - app exports HTTP on a port; doesn't rely on external web server.
8) Concurrency - scale by running more processes/instances.
9) Disposability - fast startup, graceful shutdown (handle SIGTERM).
10) Dev/prod parity - keep environments similar (same DB type, same deps).
11) Logs - write to stdout. Let the platform collect.
12) Admin processes - migrations, jobs as one-off processes against the same env.

questions.qText: Service mesh (Istio) - what does it add?
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps + Microservices: Scenario Q&A with YAML/configs

blocks.text: Service mesh is a dedicated infrastructure layer that handles service-to-service communication. Sidecar proxies (Envoy) deployed alongside each pod intercept all traffic. Features: 1) Traffic management - retries, timeouts, circuit breaking, canary routing - without code changes. 2) Security - automatic mTLS between services, identity-based authorization. 3) Observability - automatic metrics, distributed tracing, access logs across all services. 4) Policy - rate limiting, fault injection for chaos testing. Trade-off: added latency (~1-2ms per hop), operational complexity. Use when 20+ microservices, otherwise overkill.
