stage-10.sectionTitle: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

questions.qText: What is the difference between a container and a VM?
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

blocks.text: VM (Virtual Machine): full guest OS running on a hypervisor. Each VM has its own kernel, drivers, OS files. Heavy (GBs), slow to start (minutes), strong isolation.
Container: process running on the host OS kernel, isolated via Linux namespaces and cgroups. Shares host kernel. No guest OS. Light (MBs), fast to start (seconds), good but weaker isolation than VMs.
Containers are great for packaging apps with dependencies. VMs are still useful for stronger isolation, different OS kernels, legacy apps.

questions.qText: Docker image vs container?
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

blocks.text: Image: immutable template (like a class). Contains OS files, application code, dependencies, configuration. Built from a Dockerfile. Layered for efficiency. Container: running instance of an image (like an object). Has read-write layer on top. Can be started, stopped, restarted. Multiple containers can run from one image. Analogy: image is the blueprint, container is the house.

questions.qText: Why use multi-stage Docker builds?
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

blocks.text: Multi-stage builds let you use multiple FROM statements. First stage builds the app (needs JDK, Maven), final stage only contains runtime essentials (JRE, the JAR).
Example: stage 1 'FROM maven:3.9 AS build' compiles to .jar. Stage 2 'FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre' copies only the JAR. Final image is 200MB instead of 800MB.
Benefits: smaller images (faster pull, less attack surface), no build tools in production image, security.

questions.qText: What is a Pod in Kubernetes?
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

blocks.text: A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes. It's a group of one or more containers that share network (same IP, port space) and storage (volumes). Usually 1 container per pod; multi-container pods are for sidecars (logging agent, service mesh proxy). Pods are ephemeral - they get replaced, not updated in place. You don't usually create Pods directly; you create Deployments which manage Pods via ReplicaSets.

questions.qText: Difference between Deployment, StatefulSet, and DaemonSet?
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

blocks.text: Deployment: stateless apps. Pods are interchangeable - any can serve any request. Rolling updates, rollbacks. Use for web servers, APIs.
StatefulSet: stateful apps. Pods have stable identity (pod-0, pod-1), stable network names, stable storage. Ordered creation and deletion. Use for databases, Kafka, ZooKeeper.
DaemonSet: one pod per node (or matching nodes). Use for node-level services: log collectors (Fluentd), monitoring agents (Datadog), CNI plugins.

questions.qText: What is a Service in Kubernetes?
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

blocks.text: A Service is a stable network endpoint for accessing a set of Pods. Pods are ephemeral - their IPs change. Services give you a fixed DNS name and load-balance requests across healthy pods.
Types: ClusterIP (default - internal only). NodePort (exposes on every node's port - dev/testing). LoadBalancer (provisions a cloud load balancer - production). ExternalName (CNAME alias).
Services select pods by labels. For external HTTP traffic in production, use Ingress (smarter routing, TLS termination) backed by Services.

questions.qText: What is the difference between ConfigMap and Secret?
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

blocks.text: Both store key-value config that pods can consume as env vars or files. ConfigMap: plain text, non-sensitive config (feature flags, log levels, URLs). Secret: sensitive data (passwords, tokens, certificates). Stored base64-encoded - NOT encrypted by default at REST in etcd. Best practices: enable encryption at rest, use external secret managers (AWS Secrets Manager + External Secrets Operator), restrict RBAC access.

questions.qText: Explain a CI/CD pipeline for a Spring Boot app.
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

blocks.text: Typical pipeline triggered on git push:
1) Checkout code from git. 2) Cache Maven dependencies. 3) Run unit tests (mvn test). 4) Run static analysis (SpotBugs, Checkstyle, SonarQube).
5) Build JAR (mvn package -DskipTests). 6) Run integration tests with TestContainers. 7) Build Docker image. 8) Scan image for vulnerabilities (Trivy).
9) Push image to registry (ECR/Docker Hub) with git SHA tag. 10) Deploy: update Kubernetes deployment image, or trigger ArgoCD/Flux to pull new manifest.
11) Run smoke tests on deployed env. 12) Notify Slack on success/failure. Production deploys typically require manual approval.

questions.qText: Blue-green vs Canary deployment?
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

blocks.text: Blue-green: two identical environments. Blue is live. Deploy new version to Green. Switch traffic from Blue to Green (load balancer or DNS). Instant rollback by switching back. Pro: instant rollback, no downtime. Con: 2x infrastructure cost.

Canary: gradually route a small percentage of traffic to new version (e.g., 5%, then 25%, then 50%, then 100%). Monitor metrics. Rollback if error rate spikes. Pro: catches issues with real traffic, low risk. Con: more complex, requires good observability.
Modern approach: feature flags inside the code give per-user control without redeployment.

questions.qText: What is the difference between Terraform and Ansible?
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

blocks.text: Terraform: provisioning - creates infrastructure (VMs, networks, databases, K8s clusters). Declarative - you describe desired state, Terraform plans the changes. Uses state file to track resources. Cloud-agnostic via providers. Ansible: configuration management - configures EXISTING servers (install packages, edit configs, run commands). Procedural-ish (declarative with explicit ordering). Agentless (SSH-based). Often used together: Terraform creates VMs, Ansible configures them. In container/K8s world, Ansible is less needed - configs go into Docker images.

questions.qText: What is a VPC? Subnets?
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

blocks.text: VPC (Virtual Private Cloud): isolated network in AWS. You define IP range (CIDR like 10.0.0.0/16). Multi-tenant cloud, but your VPC is private to you.

Subnets: sub-ranges of the VPC CIDR, attached to an Availability Zone. Public subnet: has route to Internet Gateway. Private subnet: no direct internet, can reach internet via NAT Gateway (outbound only).
Typical setup: public subnets for load balancers and bastion. Private subnets for application servers and databases. Multiple AZs for HA.

questions.qText: Difference between security group and NACL in AWS?
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

blocks.text: Security Group: stateful - if you allow inbound, response is auto-allowed. Acts at instance/ENI level. Default deny inbound, default allow outbound. Used for fine-grained per-instance rules. NACL (Network ACL): stateless - need explicit rules for both directions. Acts at subnet level. Default allow inbound and outbound. Used for broad subnet-level rules and explicit deny rules (Security Groups can't deny). Best practice: configure Security Groups for app-level, NACLs as defense-in-depth (e.g., block known bad IPs).

questions.qText: What is AWS Lambda? When to use it?
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

blocks.text: AWS Lambda: serverless compute. You upload code, AWS runs it on demand. No servers to manage. Pay per invocation and duration. Auto-scales from 0 to thousands of concurrent executions.
Use cases: event-driven processing (S3 upload triggers thumbnail generation), API backends (with API Gateway), scheduled tasks (CloudWatch Events), ETL pipelines, IoT data processing.

Limits: 15-min max execution, 10GB memory max, cold starts (especially for JVM languages - 1-3 seconds). Not for: long-running tasks, high-concurrency low-latency APIs without provisioned concurrency.

questions.qText: Explain horizontal vs vertical scaling.
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

blocks.text: Vertical scaling (scale up): bigger server (more CPU, RAM). Simple but has limits (you can't infinitely make one machine bigger), downtime to resize, single point of failure. Horizontal scaling (scale out): more servers. Theoretically unlimited, no single point of failure, can use cheaper machines. But: requires stateless services (or external state in Redis/DB), load balancer needed, more operational complexity. Modern apps: scale horizontally for app tier, vertically (or sharded horizontally) for databases.

questions.qText: What does kubectl describe pod do? When to use it?
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

blocks.text: kubectl describe pod <pod-name>: shows detailed info about a pod - status, events, container statuses, restart count, volume mounts, environment variables, conditions. Most useful for debugging WHY a pod is failing. The Events section at the bottom is gold - shows scheduler errors, image pull failures, probe failures, OOMKilled events. Use it before kubectl logs - it tells you if the pod even started.

questions.qText: Liveness vs readiness probe?
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

blocks.text: Both are health checks for pods. Readiness probe: 'am I ready to serve traffic?'. If fails, pod is removed from Service load balancer until it passes. Liveness probe: 'am I alive or stuck?'. If fails, kubelet kills the pod and restarts it.
Example: Spring Boot app takes 30s to start. Without readiness probe, traffic hits it before it's ready - 500 errors. With readiness on /actuator/health/readiness, traffic only flows after it passes.
Liveness for detecting hung apps - app responds slowly or to /actuator/health/liveness. Don't make liveness too aggressive - it can cause crash loops.

questions.qText: What is GitOps?
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

blocks.text: GitOps: a pattern where git is the single source of truth for infrastructure and application config. Workflow: 1) Define desired state in YAML (K8s manifests, Helm charts) in a git repo. 2) An agent (ArgoCD or Flux) watches the repo and applies changes to the cluster. 3) Any change is a git commit/PR - audited, reviewed, rollback by reverting commit. Benefits: declarative, version-controlled, auditable, easy rollback. Eliminates ad-hoc kubectl commands.

questions.qText: What are 12-Factor App principles?
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

blocks.text: A methodology for cloud-native apps. Brief: 1) Codebase - one codebase, many deploys. 2) Dependencies - declare and isolate. 3) Config - in environment, not code. 4) Backing Services - treat as attached resources. 5) Build, Release, Run - separate stages. 6) Processes - stateless. 7) Port Binding - export services via ports. 8) Concurrency - scale via process model. 9) Disposability - fast startup, graceful shutdown. 10) Dev/prod parity - keep environments similar. 11) Logs - treat as event streams (to stdout). 12) Admin Processes - run as one-off processes. Following these makes apps cloud-friendly.

questions.qText: How does service discovery work in Kubernetes?
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

blocks.text: K8s built-in via CoreDNS. Every Service gets a DNS name: <service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local. Pods can resolve and call services by name. Inside the same namespace, just 'service-name' works. K8s automatically updates which pod IPs are behind the service. For service mesh (Istio, Linkerd), discovery is enhanced with load balancing, traffic splitting, retries, mTLS - all transparent to the app code.

questions.qText: What is the difference between Docker Compose and Kubernetes?
questions.header: Stage 10 - DevOps and Cloud Interview Q&A

blocks.text: Docker Compose: orchestrates multiple containers on a SINGLE host via docker-compose.yml. Great for development, simple deployments, local testing. No auto-scaling, no self-healing across nodes. Kubernetes: orchestrates containers across a CLUSTER of nodes. Handles scheduling, auto-scaling, self-healing, rolling updates, service discovery, secret management. Production-grade. Steeper learning curve. Modern path: Compose for dev, K8s for production.
This is what separates mid from senior. Take your time here - 3-6 months of consistent study. Both LLD (object design) and HLD (large-system design) are tested.
11.1 Low-Level Design (LLD)
• SOLID principles - all 5
• Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)
• Open-Closed Principle (OCP)
• Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)
• Interface Segregation Principle (ISP)
• Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)
• DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself)
• KISS (Keep It Simple, Stupid)
• YAGNI (You Aren't Gonna Need It)
• Coupling and cohesion
• Composition over inheritance

questions.qText: Explain SOLID principles with examples.

blocks.text: S - Single Responsibility: a class should have ONE reason to change. Example: separate UserService (business logic) from UserRepository (data) from UserController (HTTP).

O - Open/Closed: classes open for extension, closed for modification. Add new behavior via new subclasses or strategies, not by editing existing code. Example: PaymentProcessor interface with implementations CreditCardProcessor, UPIProcessor - add new ones without changing existing.

L - Liskov Substitution: subtypes must be substitutable for their base types without breaking behavior. Classic violation: Square extends Rectangle - setting width on a Square also changes height, breaking expectations.
I - Interface Segregation: many specific interfaces over one big one. Don't force clients to depend on methods they don't use. Example: split Printer interface into Printable, Scannable, Faxable.

D - Dependency Inversion: depend on abstractions, not concretions. High-level modules shouldn't depend on low-level modules. Both depend on interfaces. Example: OrderService depends on PaymentGateway interface, not StripeGateway directly. Enables testing with mocks.

questions.qText: Difference between Abstract Class and Interface (system design lens)?

blocks.text: Abstract Class: when you have shared CODE among related classes. Use 'is-a' relationships. Single inheritance only. Can have state. Example: AbstractAnimal with common methods Dog and Cat both extend. Interface: when you define a CAPABILITY/CONTRACT without dictating implementation. Use 'can-do' relationships. Multiple inheritance of type. Example: Printable, Serializable. Modern design: prefer interfaces (better testability, decoupling). Use abstract classes when there's genuine shared code.

questions.qText: Explain CAP theorem.

blocks.text: In a distributed system, you can have at most 2 of: Consistency (all nodes see same data at same time), Availability (every request gets a response, success or failure), Partition tolerance (system works despite network failures between nodes).
Since network partitions are inevitable in distributed systems, you really choose between C and A when a partition occurs.
CP systems: prioritize consistency. May refuse requests during partitions. Examples: traditional RDBMS clusters, MongoDB (with strong consistency), HBase.
AP systems: prioritize availability. May return stale data during partitions. Examples: DynamoDB, Cassandra, Couchbase. Eventually consistent.
Modern view: PACELC - extends CAP. Even when no partition, you trade Latency vs Consistency.

questions.qText: How would you design a URL shortener like bit.ly?

blocks.text: Requirements: shorten long URL to short code (~7 chars), redirect short to long, handle billions of URLs, low latency.
API: POST /shorten {url} -> {shortCode}. GET /:shortCode -> 301 redirect to long URL.

Generate short code: 1) Hash (MD5) of URL, take first 7 chars - collisions possible. 2) Counter-based: increment ID, encode in base62 (a-z, A-Z, 0-9 = 62 chars; 7 chars = 3.5 trillion). 3) Pre-generate codes in a pool and assign.
Storage: shortCode -> longURL mapping. SQL works at smaller scale. For massive scale, NoSQL (DynamoDB, Cassandra) sharded on shortCode.
Caching: Redis caches popular shortCodes -> longURL. 80/20 rule means cache hit ratio is high.
Read-heavy (100x more reads than writes): scale reads via cache + read replicas. CDN at edge for hot URLs.
Analytics: separate pipeline - log click events to Kafka, aggregate in batch (Spark) or stream (Flink) to a data warehouse.

questions.qText: How would you design a rate limiter?

blocks.text: Goal: prevent abuse, allow N requests per user per time window.

Algorithms: 1) Fixed window: counter per (user, minute). INCR in Redis with EXPIRE. Simple but bursty at boundary. 2) Sliding window log: store timestamps of each request, count those in window. Accurate but more memory. 3) Sliding window counter: hybrid - weighted current + previous window. Good balance. 4) Token bucket: bucket of N tokens, refills at fixed rate. Allows controlled bursts. 5) Leaky bucket: requests added to queue, processed at fixed rate.
Where to enforce: API Gateway is ideal (Kong, AWS API Gateway have it built-in). Or as middleware in app (Bucket4j + Redis for distributed).
Distributed challenge: clock skew between instances, Redis race conditions. Use Lua scripts in Redis for atomic INCR + check.

questions.qText: How would you design a chat application like WhatsApp?

blocks.text: Requirements: 1-on-1 chat, group chat, online status, read receipts, message delivery, billions of users.

Real-time: WebSocket connections from clients to chat servers. Each user has persistent connection. Load balancer needs sticky sessions or use connection routing (Redis pub/sub for cross-server messages).
Data model: Messages (msgId, fromUserId, toUserId/groupId, content, timestamp, status). User -> deviceTokens. Conversations table.
Storage: Cassandra for messages (partition by conversationId, sort by timestamp) - massive write throughput, time-range queries fast.

Message flow: User A sends message -> chat server -> persists in Cassandra -> publishes to message queue -> chat server with User B's connection delivers. If B offline, push notification via FCM/APNs.
Delivery receipts: status (sent/delivered/read) updated as message progresses. Send back ack to sender.
Scale: shard chat servers; users connect to consistent server based on userId hash. Use Kafka for cross-server delivery.

questions.qText: Explain consistent hashing.

blocks.text: Problem with simple hashing (hash(key) % N): when N changes (add/remove node), almost all keys remap. Cache becomes useless after a node change.
Consistent hashing: imagine a ring of values 0 to 2^32. Each node placed at hash(nodeId) on the ring. Each key placed at hash(key). Key is assigned to the next node clockwise.
Adding a node: only keys between previous node and new node remap (1/N of keys instead of all). Removing a node: only its keys move to next node.
Issue: uneven distribution if nodes hash to clustered positions. Fix: virtual nodes - each physical node placed at 100-200 positions on the ring.
Used in: distributed caches (Memcached client), DynamoDB, Cassandra, load balancers.

questions.qText: How would you design an LRU cache?

blocks.text: LRU = Least Recently Used. Evict the item not used for the longest time. Need O(1) get and put.
Data structure: HashMap + Doubly Linked List. HashMap: key -> node. DLL: ordered by recency, head = most recent, tail = least recent.

Get: look up node in HashMap, return value, move node to head. Put: if exists, update value, move to head. If new, create node, put at head. If size exceeds capacity, remove tail from both DLL and HashMap.
Java built-in: LinkedHashMap with accessOrder=true. Override removeEldestEntry. Or use Guava CacheBuilder. For distributed: Redis with maxmemory-policy=allkeys-lru.

questions.qText: What is event-driven architecture? Pros and cons?

blocks.text: Services communicate by emitting and reacting to events (vs synchronous REST calls). Producer publishes event to broker (Kafka, RabbitMQ); consumers subscribe.

Pros: 1) Loose coupling - producer doesn't know consumers. 2) Async - producer doesn't wait. 3) Multiple consumers can react to same event. 4) Resilience - if consumer is down, message waits. 5) Easier to add new features (new consumer).

Cons: 1) Harder to debug - flow not visible in one place. 2) Eventual consistency - state updates take time to propagate. 3) Duplicate messages possible - consumers must be idempotent. 4) Schema evolution complexity. 5) Need observability investment (distributed tracing).

questions.qText: Sharding strategies?

blocks.text: Range-based: partition by ranges of a key (user_id 1-1M on shard1, 1M-2M on shard2). Easy range queries. Risk: hotspots if traffic skewed.
Hash-based: hash(key) % N. Even distribution. But range queries hit all shards. Resharding is painful (consistent hashing helps).
Geo-based: partition by user's location. Good for location-based services. Compliance with data residency laws (GDPR).
Directory-based: a lookup service maps key to shard. Flexible (can rebalance dynamically) but lookup service is a bottleneck/SPOF.

questions.qText: How would you design a notification system?

blocks.text: Functional: support email, SMS, push, in-app. Different priorities. Rate limit per user. Personalization.
API: POST /notify {userId, channel, template, params}.

Flow: API publishes to Kafka topic 'notifications'. Consumers per channel (EmailService, SMSService, PushService) consume and call provider APIs (SES, Twilio, FCM). Retry with exponential backoff on failures. Dead-letter topic for permanent failures.
Templates: stored in DB or template service (Handlebars/Mustache). Resolve placeholders with params.
User preferences: opted out of marketing emails, preferred channel, timezone, quiet hours. Check before sending.
Rate limiting per user: prevent spamming. Bulk sending: batch and use provider's bulk APIs.
Observability: track delivery, opens, clicks. Bounce/complaint handling for emails.

questions.qText: Microservices: pros and cons?

blocks.text: Pros: independent deploy (small teams can ship without coordinating), tech diversity (Java + Node + Go in same system), fault isolation (one service down doesn't kill all), independent scaling (scale only hot services), team scaling.

Cons: operational complexity (10 deployments instead of 1), distributed tracing needed, network failures between services, data consistency (sagas, eventual consistency), latency (multiple hops), testing harder, harder to refactor across services.

Reality check: most companies should start with a well-modularized monolith and only split out specific services when they have a real reason (team size, scaling needs). 'Microservices premium' isn't worth it for small teams.

questions.qText: What is CQRS and when to use it?

blocks.text: CQRS = Command Query Responsibility Segregation. Split the model: commands (writes) use one model, queries (reads) use another.

In simple form: separate code paths for write and read. Advanced form: completely separate databases - write to normalized OLTP DB, sync to read-optimized (denormalized) DB or search index (Elasticsearch).
Use when: complex reads with many views (dashboards, reports), reads vastly outnumber writes, different scaling needs for read/write, complex domain logic on write side benefits from focused model.
Don't use: simple CRUD apps. The complexity isn't justified.

questions.qText: What is event sourcing?

blocks.text: Event sourcing: instead of storing current state, store the SEQUENCE of EVENTS that led to it. Current state is derived by replaying events. Example: bank account doesn't store balance directly; it stores Deposit and Withdraw events. Balance = sum of events. Pros: full audit log, time travel (state at any point), event replay for new projections. Cons: complexity, queries require projections (read models), event schema evolution challenging. Often combined with CQRS - events on write side, projections for read side.

questions.qText: What is a circuit breaker pattern?

blocks.text: Pattern to prevent cascading failures in distributed systems. When a downstream service repeatedly fails, the circuit breaker 'opens' and short-circuits subsequent calls - they fail fast instead of waiting for timeouts. Three states: CLOSED (normal), OPEN (failing fast), HALF_OPEN (testing recovery). Implementation: Resilience4j in Java. Configure failure threshold (e.g., 50% failure rate over 10 requests), wait duration, half-open trial requests. Combine with fallback method for graceful degradation.

questions.qText: Distributed transactions - how to handle?

blocks.text: First option: AVOID. Design to keep transactional consistency within one service/database.

When unavoidable, options: 1) Two-phase commit (2PC): coordinator asks all participants to 'prepare', then 'commit' or 'rollback'. Strong consistency but blocking, slow, doesn't scale, coordinator is SPOF. Rarely used in microservices.
2) Saga pattern: sequence of local transactions, each with a compensating action. Choreography (services react to each other's events) or Orchestration (central orchestrator). Eventually consistent.
3) Outbox pattern: write event to an outbox table in same DB transaction as business write. A separate process publishes outbox events to broker. Ensures consistency between DB and broker.
4) Idempotency keys: client retries safely without duplicate effects.

questions.qText: How to handle 10K concurrent users on a server?

blocks.text: Layered approach: 1) CDN for static assets - offload load. 2) Load balancer in front of multiple app instances. 3) Horizontal scaling of stateless app servers (10-20 instances). 4) Database connection pool tuned (HikariCP, sized to ~CPU count, not 1000). 5) Caching: Redis for hot data, reduce DB hits. 6) Read replicas for read-heavy workloads. 7) Async processing for non-critical work (queue + worker). 8) For very high concurrency: virtual threads (Java 21+) or reactive (WebFlux). 9) Profile under load with tools like JMeter, k6, Gatling to find actual bottleneck before scaling.

questions.qText: Eventual vs strong consistency. Real examples.

blocks.text: Strong consistency: after a write, all reads see the new value immediately. Example: bank account balance - you can't show stale balance to a user paying. RDBMS, MongoDB (strong mode), HBase.

Eventual consistency: after a write, reads may see old value for a while, but eventually all converge. Example: like count on a tweet - showing 1,001 vs 1,002 likes for a few seconds is fine. DynamoDB (default), Cassandra, DNS.
Choose based on the data: money, inventory -> strong. Counters, search results, recommendations -> eventual.

questions.qText: How does Kafka achieve high throughput?

blocks.text: 1) Append-only log on disk - sequential writes are fast, often faster than random writes to memory. 2) Zero-copy via sendfile() syscall - data goes directly from disk to network buffer, bypassing user space. 3) Partitioning - parallelism across partitions. 4) Batching - producers batch messages; consumers fetch batches. 5) Compression (snappy, lz4, zstd) at batch level. 6) Page cache (OS) for reads - hot data served from RAM without explicit caching. 7) No per-message acks - acks per batch. Result: millions of messages per second per broker.

questions.qText: What is the bulkhead pattern?

blocks.text: Bulkhead pattern: isolate parts of a system so that failure in one doesn't sink the whole ship (named after watertight compartments on ships). Implementation: dedicate separate thread pools / resources per downstream dependency. If Service A is slow, only its thread pool is exhausted - calls to Service B continue working. Resilience4j Bulkhead component implements this. Without bulkhead, a slow downstream can exhaust the shared thread pool and make ALL endpoints unresponsive.

stage-11.sectionTitle: System Design: Q&A with architecture diagrams

questions.qText: Design a URL Shortener (TinyURL / bit.ly) - END TO END
questions.header: Stage 11 - System Design: Deep dive Q&A with architecture diagrams

blocks.text: Step 1 - Requirements
Functional: 1) POST /shorten -> short URL. 2) GET /:code -> 301/302 redirect. 3) Optional: custom alias, expiration, click analytics, user accounts.
Non-functional: high availability (>= 99.99%), low latency (<100ms p99 redirect), highly scalable (100M+ URLs), durable (don't lose URLs).
Step 2 - Scale estimates
Assume 100M new URLs/month, 100:1 read-to-write ratio.
Writes: 100M / (30*24*3600) ~ 40 writes/sec.
Reads: 4000 reads/sec average. Peak 10x = 40,000 reads/sec.
Storage: 100M/month x 60 months = 6B records. Each ~500 bytes = 3 TB. Plus indexes.
Bandwidth: 40K reads x 500 bytes = 20 MB/sec (manageable with CDN).
Step 3 - Short code design
Length 7 chars in base62 (a-z, A-Z, 0-9) = 62^7 = 3.5 trillion combinations. Enough for decades.
Approaches: 1) Counter + base62 encode. Pro: no collisions, fast. Con: predictable URLs, single counter is bottleneck (solve with sharded counters or batch ranges from a service).
2) Hash (MD5/SHA) of URL, take first 7 chars. Con: collisions need handling; same URL = same short code (good or bad?).
3) Pre-generate codes in a pool; pluck one on each write.
Step 4 - Architecture diagram
Step 5 - Database choice
Schema is simple: (short_code PRIMARY KEY, long_url, created_at, user_id, expires_at).
Options: 1) PostgreSQL/MySQL with read replicas - works up to billions of rows if sharded. 2) DynamoDB - managed, auto-scaling. 3) Cassandra - excellent write throughput, sharded natively.
Sharding: by short_code (hash). Read-heavy -> add read replicas. Cassandra natively partitions by key.
Step 6 - Caching
Redis caches hot URLs (Pareto: 20% of URLs get 80% of traffic). TTL = 1 hour. Cache-aside pattern. Hit ratio should be 90%+.
CDN edge caches GET /:code with appropriate Cache-Control headers. Even better for global latency.
Step 7 - Analytics
On redirect, publish click event to Kafka with: short_code, user_agent, referer, IP, timestamp. Separate consumer aggregates into time-series DB (ClickHouse, InfluxDB, BigQuery).
Do NOT make analytics writes synchronous - they would slow down redirects.
Step 8 - Trade-offs

301 (permanent) vs 302 (temporary) redirect: 301 lets browsers cache forever (we don't get the next click - hurts analytics). 302 means every click reaches our servers (analytics work, more load). bit.ly uses 301 with caching headers controlled, or sometimes 302 for tracking.
Counter approach uses contiguous integers - allows enumeration of all URLs. If privacy matters, use random codes or encrypt the counter.

questions.qText: Design WhatsApp / chat application - 1-on-1 and group messaging
questions.header: Stage 11 - System Design: Deep dive Q&A with architecture diagrams

blocks.text: Requirements
Functional: send/receive messages, group chat, online status, typing indicator, delivery receipts (sent/delivered/read), media (image/video), end-to-end encryption.
Non-functional: billions of users, low latency, very high availability, message ordering preserved, encryption.
Scale estimates
2B users, 50 messages/user/day = 100B msgs/day = 1.2M msgs/sec average. Peak 5x. Storage: 100B msgs x 500 bytes = 50 TB/day.
Architecture diagram

lines:
C[Mobile / Web Client] -- WebSocket --> LB[Load Balancer
sticky session]

blocks.text: Key design choices
WebSocket connections: each user has a persistent WS to a chat server. Use a connection registry (Redis) mapping userId -> server hosting them.

Message flow: User A sends -> chat server -> persist message in Cassandra -> publish on Kafka with recipient(s) -> recipient's chat server receives, forwards over WS. If recipient offline, push notification.
Cassandra schema: messages table partitioned by chat_id, clustering by timestamp DESC. Each chat is one partition - fast scan for chat history. Pagination by timestamp.

Group chats: 'fan out on write' for small groups (copy message to each recipient's mailbox). 'Fan out on read' for huge groups (one copy, all readers fetch from same partition). Hybrid: write once, fan out via Kafka for active members.
Presence: Redis with TTL heartbeats. Client sends heartbeat every 30s. Online status expires if no heartbeat in 60s.
Delivery receipts: separate small status events flowing back through same pipeline.
E2E encryption (Signal Protocol): keys generated on device, server only forwards encrypted bytes. Server can't read messages.

questions.qText: Design Twitter / X feed
questions.header: Stage 11 - System Design: Deep dive Q&A with architecture diagrams

blocks.text: Requirements
Post tweets, follow users, view home feed (tweets of users you follow), like/retweet/reply, search, trending topics.
Scale: 500M users, 200M daily active, 500M tweets/day = 6K tweets/sec average. Feed reads dominate - 100B+ reads/day.
Architecture
Key trade-off: Fan-out on WRITE vs READ
Fan-out on write (Twitter's default): when a user tweets, push the tweet ID into each follower's pre-computed timeline (Redis list). Feed read = single Redis call. FAST.

Problem: celebrities with 100M followers create write storms. Solution: HYBRID - for top users (>1M followers), don't fan out; their tweets are fetched at read time and merged. Most users use fan-out, celebs use pull.
Fan-out on read: at read time, query tweets of all people you follow, merge, sort. Simple but slow for users following thousands.
Tweet ID generation: Snowflake-style 64-bit IDs (timestamp + machine_id + sequence). Roughly sortable, distributed.
Search: Elasticsearch indexes tweets by text, hashtags, mentions. Updated via Kafka.
Trending: real-time analytics pipeline (Kafka + Spark Streaming or Flink). Top hashtags by region, sliding window.

questions.qText: Design Uber / Ride-sharing service
questions.header: Stage 11 - System Design: Deep dive Q&A with architecture diagrams

blocks.text: Requirements
Rider opens app, sees nearby drivers; requests ride; gets matched; sees driver location in real-time; pays. Driver receives request, accepts, navigates, completes.
Scale: 100M users, 1M concurrent rides, location updates every 4 seconds per active driver.
Architecture

lines: Driver[Driver App] -- GPS every 4s --> Loc[Location Service]

blocks.text: Location updates and matching
Driver app sends location every 4s via WebSocket or HTTP. Server stores in-memory geo index (S2 cells or QuadTree). Redis GEO commands work for moderate scale (GEOADD, GEOSEARCH).

Matching: when rider requests, find drivers within radius (e.g., 5 km). Send request to nearest available driver. If declined or timeout, expand to next nearest. Multi-driver broadcast for premium services to reduce wait time.
Trip lifecycle: requested -> matched -> driver arrived -> in progress -> completed. Each state change publishes to Kafka for downstream services (notification, pricing, analytics).
Surge pricing: real-time analytics on demand/supply ratio per geo cell. Updates pricing every few minutes.

questions.qText: Design Netflix / Video streaming
questions.header: Stage 11 - System Design: Deep dive Q&A with architecture diagrams

blocks.text: Requirements
Users browse catalog, search, watch videos with adaptive bitrate, recommendations, watch history.
Scale: 270M+ subscribers, peak concurrent streams in tens of millions. Petabytes of video data. Global distribution.
Architecture

lines:
User[User Device] --> CDN[CDN edge
HLS / DASH chunks]

blocks.text: Key insights
Open Connect: Netflix's own CDN. Servers deployed inside ISPs (so traffic stays local). Pre-positioned content - they predict what people will watch and seed it overnight.

Adaptive bitrate streaming (ABR / HLS / DASH): video chunked into 2-10 second segments at multiple bitrates. Client measures bandwidth and picks appropriate bitrate per chunk. Seamless quality switching.
Transcoding: when content arrives, transcoded to dozens of variants (different resolutions, codecs - H.264, H.265, AV1) for diverse devices.
Microservices: famously 1000+ microservices. Each team owns its services. Communicate via Kafka and gRPC.
Chaos Engineering: pioneered Chaos Monkey - randomly kill instances in production to verify resilience.
Recommendations: massive ML pipeline. Personalized via collaborative filtering + content embeddings + watch context. A/B tested constantly.

questions.qText: Design Instagram / photo sharing
questions.header: Stage 11 - System Design: Deep dive Q&A with architecture diagrams

blocks.text: Requirements
Users post photos, follow, view feed, like, comment, search hashtags, view profiles.
Scale: 2B users, billions of photos, read-heavy (like 100:1).
Architecture
Photo upload pipeline

1) Client uploads original. 2) Service stores in S3, creates Post record. 3) Async pipeline: generate thumbnails (multiple sizes), apply ML for content moderation, extract EXIF, generate hash for duplicate detection. 4) Push post to feed pipeline.
Feed = same fan-out-on-write as Twitter for normal users; pull for huge accounts.
Photos served via CDN with cache headers - immutable URLs (S3 key includes hash). Multiple thumbnail sizes served based on device.

questions.qText: Design a Rate Limiter (distributed)
questions.header: Stage 11 - System Design: Deep dive Q&A with architecture diagrams

blocks.text: Algorithm options
1) Token bucket: bucket holds N tokens. Each request takes one. Refills at fixed rate. Allows bursts up to N. Most common.
2) Leaky bucket: requests put in queue, processed at fixed rate. Smooths bursts. Acts as both queue and limiter.
3) Fixed window counter: N requests per X seconds. Simple but bursty at window boundary (2N possible at boundary).
4) Sliding window log: store all request timestamps in last X seconds, count them. Accurate but memory-heavy.
5) Sliding window counter: hybrid - weighted current + previous window. Good balance, low memory.
Distributed implementation (Redis-based)

lines: -- Atomic Lua script for sliding window counter

blocks.text: Where to enforce
1) API Gateway (Kong, AWS API Gateway): centralized, language-agnostic.
2) Application middleware: more flexible per-endpoint logic but needs to be in every service.
3) Sidecar (Envoy, Istio): in service mesh.
Architecture

lines:
GW -- check counter --> R[(Redis Cluster
token bucket / sliding window)]

questions.qText: Design a Notification System (email, SMS, push)
questions.header: Stage 11 - System Design: Deep dive Q&A with architecture diagrams

blocks.text: Requirements
Send through email/SMS/push/in-app. Templated. Localized. User preferences. Schedule. Retry on failure. Track delivery. Rate limit per user.
Architecture
Key design decisions
Decouple via Kafka: API just publishes; workers process asynchronously. Bursts buffered in Kafka. Workers can be scaled per channel.
Templates: stored in DB with placeholders. Versioned. Localized. Render at consume time with personalization data.
User preferences: opted-out channels, quiet hours, language, frequency caps - checked before publishing.
Retries: workers retry with exponential backoff. Permanent failures (invalid email) move to DLT for inspection.
Tracking: delivery, open, click events flow back. Aggregate per template/segment for analytics.
Rate limit per user: prevent flood (e.g., max 5 emails/day per user).

questions.qText: Design a Payment Processing System
questions.header: Stage 11 - System Design: Deep dive Q&A with architecture diagrams

blocks.text: Requirements
Process card payments, support multiple gateways (Stripe, Razorpay), handle refunds, idempotency (no double charges), reconciliation, fraud detection, audit trail (regulatory).
Constraints: must be ACID for money operations. PCI-DSS compliance (don't store raw card numbers).
Architecture
Critical patterns
Idempotency: every charge request has a client-provided Idempotency-Key (UUID). Server checks Redis - if seen, return cached response. Prevents duplicate charges on retries (network issues).
State machine: payments go pending -> processing -> succeeded/failed. State transitions logged.
Saga for refunds across services: refund payment -> mark order refunded -> notify customer. Each step compensable.
Webhooks (gateway -> us): verify HMAC signature, process exactly-once (deduplicate by event ID), respond 200 fast (process async).
Tokenize cards: never store raw PAN. Use gateway's tokens (vault). Only last 4 digits stored for display.
Reconciliation: scheduled job compares our DB with gateway's daily statement. Discrepancies are flagged for human review.

questions.qText: Design a recommendation / news feed ranking system
questions.header: Stage 11 - System Design: Deep dive Q&A with architecture diagrams

blocks.text: Approach

Pipeline: 1) Candidate generation - retrieve ~1000 candidate items (followers' posts, trending, related). 2) Ranking - ML model scores each candidate by predicted engagement (CTR/dwell-time). 3) Re-ranking - apply diversity rules, sponsored content slots, fatigue (don't show same post type repeatedly).
Architecture

questions.qText: Design search autocomplete (typeahead)
questions.header: Stage 11 - System Design: Deep dive Q&A with architecture diagrams

blocks.text: Requirements: as user types, suggest popular completions. Latency < 100ms. Updates as new queries become popular.
Architecture

lines:
Q[/Kafka: search queries/] --> Agg[Aggregator
count + time decay]

blocks.text: Key details
Trie data structure: each node stores top-K (e.g., top 10) completions for its prefix, pre-computed. Query is O(prefix_length) traversal.
Hot suggestions cached in Redis with TTL.
Updates: real-time approximate (HyperLogLog for counts) merged with periodic full rebuild.
Personalization: filter/rerank by user history and region.

questions.qText: Design a Distributed Cache (Redis-like)
questions.header: Stage 11 - System Design: Deep dive Q&A with architecture diagrams

blocks.text: Requirements: in-memory key-value store, distributed across many nodes, low latency, high throughput, support TTL, eviction.
Key decisions
Distribution: consistent hashing maps keys to nodes. Adding/removing a node shifts only ~1/N keys.
Replication: each shard replicated 2-3 times. Primary handles writes, replicas serve reads.
Eviction: LRU / LFU when memory hits limit. Per-key TTL for cache use.
Consistency: typically eventually consistent (async replication). For stronger guarantees, sync replication at write latency cost.
Architecture

lines: Sent[Sentinel / Cluster Manager] -.health.-> N1 & N2 & N3

questions.qText: Design Dropbox / Google Drive (file sync and share)
questions.header: Stage 11 - System Design: Deep dive Q&A with architecture diagrams

blocks.text: Requirements: upload/download files, sync across devices, share with others, version history, conflict resolution.
Architecture
Smart sync
Files split into fixed-size blocks (chunks). Each chunk identified by content hash. Same chunk uploaded once globally (deduplication). When file changes, only changed blocks are transferred.
Versions: keep block references for each version. Old versions can be restored without storing full file.
Conflict: last-write-wins by default; create 'conflicted copy' files when client and server diverge.

questions.qText: Design a Distributed Job Scheduler (like cron at scale)
questions.header: Stage 11 - System Design: Deep dive Q&A with architecture diagrams

blocks.text: Requirements: schedule jobs (cron-style or one-off). Distributed - jobs run on a fleet of workers. Resilient - jobs not lost if worker dies. Visibility - track status, retries, history.
Architecture

lines:
Sub[Submit Job API] --> Sched[(Scheduler DB
jobs · next_run_at)]

Picker[Picker Workers
SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED] --> Sched

blocks.text: Key patterns
Idempotency: jobs must be safe to run multiple times (worker may crash mid-job, scheduler retries).
Leader election: prevents duplicate dispatch. Use ZooKeeper / etcd / Redis.
Visibility timeout (SQS pattern): worker grabs job, has N minutes to ack. If not, job becomes visible again.
Retries with backoff: max attempts, exponential backoff, final dead-letter for human review.

questions.qText: Design a Web Crawler (like Googlebot)
questions.header: Stage 11 - System Design: Deep dive Q&A with architecture diagrams

blocks.text: Requirements: crawl billions of pages, respect robots.txt, detect duplicates, prioritize freshness, distributed.
Architecture
Key challenges
Politeness: don't hammer one site. Throttle per host. Respect robots.txt and Crawl-Delay.
Deduplication: identical pages at different URLs (canonical link), near-duplicates (SimHash).
URL frontier priority: importance (PageRank), freshness needs (news vs static).
Distributed coordination: shard URLs by host hash so politeness is enforceable.

questions.qText: What happens when you type google.com in the browser and press Enter?

blocks.text: Step 1: Browser checks its cache for the DNS record of google.com. If not found, it asks the OS, then the local resolver, then the recursive DNS resolver (like 8.8.8.8), which queries root, TLD (.com), and authoritative name servers to get the IP address.
Step 2: With the IP, the browser opens a TCP connection on port 443 (HTTPS) using the 3-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK).
Step 3: TLS handshake happens to establish encryption (cert verification, key exchange).
Step 4: Browser sends an HTTP GET request over the secure connection.
Step 5: Server processes the request, may hit load balancers, app servers, databases, and returns HTML response.
Step 6: Browser parses HTML, requests CSS/JS/images (more HTTP requests), builds the DOM, and renders the page.

questions.qText: What is the difference between a process and a thread?

blocks.text: A process is an independent program in execution with its own memory space, file handles, and resources. A thread is the smallest unit of execution inside a process. A process can have multiple threads that share the same memory space and resources but each has its own stack and program counter.

Key difference: Processes are isolated (one crashing doesn't kill others). Threads share memory (faster to communicate but one bad thread can corrupt the whole process). Creating a process is heavy; creating a thread is light.

questions.qText: What is the difference between TCP and UDP?

blocks.text: TCP is connection-oriented and reliable. It establishes a connection (3-way handshake), guarantees order, retransmits lost packets, and handles flow control. Used for HTTP, email, file transfer.

UDP is connectionless and unreliable. It just sends packets without confirmation, no order guarantee, no retransmission. But it's much faster and lighter. Used for video streaming, online games, DNS, VoIP - cases where speed matters more than reliability.

questions.qText: What is the difference between HTTP and HTTPS?

blocks.text: HTTP sends data in plain text on port 80. HTTPS sends encrypted data on port 443 using TLS/SSL. HTTPS verifies the server's identity using digital certificates issued by trusted Certificate Authorities. Without HTTPS, anyone on the network can read passwords, credit cards, and session tokens. Today every public website should use HTTPS.

questions.qText: Explain HTTP status codes you use most often.

blocks.text: 200 OK - successful request. 201 Created - resource created (used after POST). 204 No Content - successful but nothing to return (used after DELETE).
301 Moved Permanently, 302 Found - redirects. 304 Not Modified - cached version is still valid.

400 Bad Request - invalid syntax. 401 Unauthorized - not authenticated. 403 Forbidden - authenticated but not allowed. 404 Not Found - resource doesn't exist. 409 Conflict - state conflict. 422 Unprocessable Entity - validation failed. 429 Too Many Requests - rate limited.

500 Internal Server Error - generic server failure. 502 Bad Gateway - upstream server bad response. 503 Service Unavailable - server overloaded or down. 504 Gateway Timeout - upstream didn't respond in time.

questions.qText: What is the difference between GET and POST?

blocks.text: GET retrieves data; POST sends data to create something. GET parameters are in the URL (visible, bookmarkable, limited size). POST data is in the request body (hidden, larger size allowed).

GET is idempotent (calling it 5 times gives same result). POST is not - calling it 5 times creates 5 resources. GET responses are cached by browsers; POST responses generally are not. GET should never modify data; POST is for modifications.

questions.qText: What is DNS and why is it needed?

blocks.text: DNS (Domain Name System) is the internet's phone book. It translates human-readable domain names (google.com) into IP addresses (142.250.183.46) that computers actually use to route traffic. Without DNS, we'd have to memorize IP addresses for every website.

questions.qText: Difference between stack and heap memory?

blocks.text: Stack: stores local variables, method calls, and primitive values. Memory is allocated and freed automatically when methods return (LIFO order). Fast access. Limited size. Each thread has its own stack.

Heap: stores objects and instance variables. Memory is shared across threads. Managed by the Garbage Collector in languages like Java. Slower access than stack. Much larger size. If you allocate too many objects, you get OutOfMemoryError.

questions.qText: What is the OSI model?

blocks.text: OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) is a 7-layer conceptual model for network communication:

Layer 7 Application (HTTP, FTP, DNS), Layer 6 Presentation (encryption, encoding), Layer 5 Session (managing sessions), Layer 4 Transport (TCP, UDP), Layer 3 Network (IP, routing), Layer 2 Data Link (Ethernet, MAC), Layer 1 Physical (cables, signals).
In practice, the TCP/IP 4-layer model is what you use: Application, Transport, Internet, Link. Interviewers ask this to test fundamentals.

questions.qText: What is the difference between a compiler and an interpreter?

blocks.text: A compiler translates the entire source code into machine code (or bytecode) before execution - produces a separate output file. Examples: C, Go. Faster execution but slower iteration. An interpreter reads and executes the source code line by line at runtime. Examples: Python, JavaScript (originally). Slower execution but faster iteration. Java uses BOTH - compiles to bytecode (compiler), and the JVM interprets/JIT-compiles bytecode at runtime.

questions.qText: What are read, write, execute permissions in Linux?

blocks.text: Every file has permissions for three groups: owner (u), group (g), others (o). Three permissions per group: read (r=4), write (w=2), execute (x=1).

Common chmod values: 755 = owner can read/write/execute, group and others can read/execute (typical for scripts). 644 = owner read/write, others read (typical for text files). 600 = owner read/write only (for secrets like SSH keys). 777 = everyone full access (almost always wrong in production).

questions.qText: What is a REST API?

blocks.text: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for web APIs. Key principles: stateless (each request contains all info needed), resource-based URLs (/users/123 not /getUser?id=123), uses standard HTTP methods (GET to read, POST to create, PUT/PATCH to update, DELETE to remove), returns standard HTTP status codes, typically uses JSON for data exchange. REST APIs make services that are simple, scalable, and language-agnostic.

questions.qText: Why is Java called platform independent?

blocks.text: Java source code (.java) is compiled to bytecode (.class), which is not specific to any operating system. Any machine with a JVM can run that bytecode. So 'write once, run anywhere' (WORA) - the bytecode is platform independent, the JVM is platform dependent.

questions.qText: What is the difference between JDK, JRE, and JVM?

blocks.text: JVM (Java Virtual Machine) is the engine that runs bytecode. It's platform-specific.
JRE (Java Runtime Environment) is JVM + core libraries needed to run Java programs. You install JRE on machines that only run Java apps, not compile them.

JDK (Java Development Kit) is JRE + development tools (javac, javadoc, jar, jdb). Developers install JDK. Since Java 11, separate JRE distribution was discontinued; you typically just install the JDK.

questions.qText: What are the 4 pillars of OOP? Explain with examples.

blocks.text: Encapsulation: bundling data and methods that operate on that data, and hiding internal state. Example: a class with private fields and public getters/setters.
Inheritance: a class deriving properties from another. Example: class Dog extends Animal - Dog inherits properties of Animal.
Polymorphism: same method behaves differently based on context. Compile-time: method overloading. Runtime: method overriding. Example: Animal a = new Dog(); a.makeSound() calls Dog's version.
Abstraction: showing only essential features and hiding implementation. Example: List interface hides whether you're using ArrayList or LinkedList. Achieved via abstract classes and interfaces.

questions.qText: Why is String immutable in Java?

blocks.text: Security: strings are used for passwords, URLs, class names. If mutable, malicious code could change them after validation.
String pool optimization: Java keeps a pool of String literals in heap. Multiple references can share the same object only if immutable.
Thread safety: immutable objects are inherently thread-safe - no synchronization needed.
HashCode caching: Strings cache their hashCode on creation, making them excellent HashMap keys. Possible only because the value never changes.
Class loading: class loader uses String for class names. If mutable, you could load a different class than intended.

questions.qText: Difference between String, StringBuilder, and StringBuffer?

blocks.text: String: immutable, every modification creates a new object. Slow for many concatenations. Thread-safe (immutable).
StringBuilder: mutable, faster, NOT thread-safe. Use in single-threaded code (most cases).
StringBuffer: mutable, thread-safe (all methods synchronized), but slower than StringBuilder due to synchronization overhead. Rarely needed today.
Rule of thumb: String for fixed text, StringBuilder for building strings in a single thread.

questions.qText: How does HashMap work internally? (Very frequently asked)

blocks.text: HashMap stores key-value pairs in an array of buckets (Node[] table, default size 16). Each bucket is the head of a linked list (or Red-Black tree since Java 8 if it gets too long).

Put operation: 1) Compute hash of the key using key.hashCode() and apply a supplemental hash (h ^ (h >>> 16)) for better distribution. 2) Find bucket index using (n - 1) & hash. 3) If bucket empty, place node. 4) If bucket has entries, walk the chain - if a node with equal key found, replace value; else append. 5) If chain length exceeds 8 AND table size >= 64, the chain is treeified into a Red-Black tree (O(log n) lookup instead of O(n)).
Get operation: compute hash, find bucket, walk chain/tree using equals() to find matching key.
Resize: when size exceeds threshold (capacity * load factor, default 0.75), capacity doubles and all entries are rehashed.
HashMap is NOT thread-safe - use ConcurrentHashMap in multi-threaded environments.

questions.qText: Why is overriding hashCode() necessary when you override equals()?

blocks.text: Contract: if two objects are equal (equals() returns true), their hashCodes MUST be equal. If you override equals() but not hashCode(), you break this contract.

Real-world impact: HashMap and HashSet use hashCode() to find the bucket, then equals() to find the exact match within that bucket. If equal objects produce different hashCodes, they land in different buckets, and lookup fails - you can put an object and never find it again.

questions.qText: What is the difference between == and .equals() in Java?

blocks.text: For primitives, == compares values directly. For objects, == compares references (memory addresses) - true only if both variables point to the same object. .equals() compares content/values based on how the class implements it. Object's default equals() is the same as ==, but classes like String, Integer, etc., override it to compare content. Always use .equals() for object content comparison, especially for Strings.

questions.qText: What is the difference between ArrayList and LinkedList?

blocks.text: ArrayList: backed by a dynamic array. Fast random access O(1). Slow insertion/deletion in middle O(n) (requires shifting elements). Better cache locality.
LinkedList: doubly linked list. Slow random access O(n). Fast insertion/deletion at known node O(1). But finding the node is O(n). More memory overhead per element (pointers).
Real world: ArrayList wins almost always. LinkedList is rarely the right choice - prefer ArrayDeque for queue operations.

questions.qText: What is the difference between Comparable and Comparator?

blocks.text: Comparable is implemented by the class itself - it defines the 'natural ordering'. Method: compareTo(T other). Example: String, Integer implement Comparable.

Comparator is a separate class/lambda used to define custom orderings without modifying the original class. Method: compare(T o1, T o2). Example: sort employees by salary, then by name - use a Comparator.
Use Comparable when there's one natural way. Use Comparator when you need multiple sort orders or can't modify the class.

questions.qText: Checked vs Unchecked exceptions?

blocks.text: Checked exceptions: subclasses of Exception (excluding RuntimeException). Compiler forces you to handle them (try/catch or throws). Used for recoverable conditions. Examples: IOException, SQLException.

Unchecked exceptions: RuntimeException and its subclasses. Not enforced by compiler. Usually indicate programming bugs. Examples: NullPointerException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException, IllegalArgumentException.
Errors: serious problems your code shouldn't catch. Examples: OutOfMemoryError, StackOverflowError.

questions.qText: What is the difference between final, finally, and finalize?

blocks.text: final: keyword used to make a variable constant, a method non-overridable, or a class non-extensible.
finally: block in try-catch that always executes (except System.exit or JVM crash). Used for cleanup.
finalize(): method in Object class called by GC before object destruction. Deprecated since Java 9, removed in newer versions. Don't use it - use try-with-resources or Cleaner instead.

questions.qText: What is the difference between abstract class and interface?

blocks.text: Abstract class: can have constructors, instance variables, both abstract and concrete methods. A class can extend only ONE abstract class. Used when you have a base class with some default implementation.

Interface: no constructors, only constants (public static final). Before Java 8: only abstract methods. Java 8+: can have default and static methods. Java 9+: can have private methods too. A class can implement MULTIPLE interfaces. Used to define a contract / capability.
When to use which: abstract class for 'is-a' with shared code. Interface for 'can-do' capabilities or when you need multiple inheritance of type.

questions.qText: What is method overloading vs overriding?

blocks.text: Overloading: same method name, different parameters (different number, types, or order) in the SAME class. Resolved at compile time (compile-time polymorphism). Return type alone is not enough to overload.

Overriding: same method signature in a SUBCLASS, providing a different implementation. Resolved at runtime (runtime polymorphism). Rules: same signature, same/covariant return type, can't reduce visibility, can't throw broader checked exceptions.

questions.qText: What is the volatile keyword?

blocks.text: volatile tells the JVM that a variable can be modified by multiple threads. Reads always come from main memory (not CPU cache), writes go directly to main memory. It provides VISIBILITY guarantees but NOT atomicity. Example: a flag like 'running' that one thread sets and another reads. For compound operations like i++, volatile is NOT enough - use AtomicInteger or synchronization.

questions.qText: What is the synchronized keyword?

blocks.text: synchronized provides mutual exclusion - only one thread can execute a synchronized block on a given lock at a time. Used to prevent race conditions. Can be applied to: instance methods (locks 'this'), static methods (locks the Class object), or blocks (locks any specified object). Provides both atomicity and visibility. Performance cost: contention serializes threads.

questions.qText: Difference between HashMap and ConcurrentHashMap?

blocks.text: HashMap: NOT thread-safe. Concurrent modifications can cause infinite loops (during resize, in older Java versions) or data corruption. Allows one null key and multiple null values.

ConcurrentHashMap: thread-safe via fine-grained locking. In Java 8+, uses CAS for empty buckets and synchronized blocks at node level for non-empty buckets - high concurrency. Does NOT allow null keys or values. Iterator is fail-safe (weakly consistent).
Use ConcurrentHashMap whenever map is shared across threads. Hashtable (the old sync map) is obsolete - it locks the entire map for every operation.

questions.qText: What is the difference between throw and throws?

blocks.text: throw is a keyword used to manually throw an exception from inside a method or block: 'throw new IllegalArgumentException("bad input")'. throws is used in a method signature to declare that the method MIGHT throw certain checked exceptions, forcing callers to handle them: 'public void readFile() throws IOException { ... }'.

questions.qText: What is the difference between fail-fast and fail-safe iterators?

blocks.text: Fail-fast iterators throw ConcurrentModificationException if the collection is modified during iteration (except via the iterator's own remove method). Examples: ArrayList, HashMap, HashSet iterators. They detect modification via a modCount field. Fail-safe iterators work on a copy/snapshot and don't throw, but may not reflect recent changes. Examples: ConcurrentHashMap, CopyOnWriteArrayList iterators.

questions.qText: What is the difference between Stream and Collection?

blocks.text: Collection is a data structure that holds elements in memory. Stream is a sequence of elements that supports aggregate operations. Streams don't store data - they pull from a source. Streams are lazy (intermediate ops don't execute until a terminal op is called). Streams can be parallel for performance. Streams are consumed once - you can't reuse them. Use Collections for storage, Streams for processing.

questions.qText: What is the use of Optional?

blocks.text: Optional is a container that may or may not contain a value. It exists to make 'absent' values explicit in APIs and to reduce NullPointerExceptions. Methods: of(), ofNullable(), empty(), isPresent(), ifPresent(), orElse(), orElseGet(), orElseThrow(), map(), flatMap(), filter(). Best practice: use Optional as return type, NOT as a field or method parameter (just adds noise).

questions.qText: What are functional interfaces in Java 8?

blocks.text: A functional interface has exactly ONE abstract method (can have default and static methods too). Marked with @FunctionalInterface annotation (optional but recommended). Used as targets for lambdas and method references.
Built-in examples: Runnable (run), Callable (call), Comparator (compare), Function<T,R> (apply), Predicate<T> (test), Consumer<T> (accept), Supplier<T> (get), BiFunction<T,U,R> (apply).

questions.qText: What is a lambda expression?

blocks.text: A lambda is an anonymous function - a way to pass behavior as data. Syntax: (params) -> expression OR (params) -> { statements; }. It implements a functional interface. Example: 'list.forEach(x -> System.out.println(x))' instead of writing an anonymous inner class with a Consumer. Lambdas enable functional programming style in Java.

questions.qText: Explain the Stream API.

blocks.text: Stream API is a way to process collections of data using a declarative pipeline of operations. Pipeline: source (collection, array, stream) -> intermediate operations (filter, map, sorted) -> terminal operation (collect, forEach, reduce, count). Streams are lazy - nothing happens until terminal op. Streams can be parallelized with parallelStream(). Example: 'employees.stream().filter(e -> e.getAge() > 30).map(Employee::getName).collect(toList())' gets names of employees over 30.

questions.qText: What is Garbage Collection? Explain how it works.

blocks.text: GC is the JVM's automatic memory management. Programmer doesn't explicitly free memory (no 'delete' like C++). GC identifies unreachable objects and reclaims their memory.

Mark and Sweep: 1) Mark phase - traverse from GC roots (static fields, local variables on stack, JNI refs) and mark all reachable objects. 2) Sweep phase - reclaim unmarked memory. Compact phase may follow to reduce fragmentation.

Generational hypothesis: most objects die young. JVM divides heap into Young Gen (Eden + 2 Survivor spaces) and Old Gen. Minor GC cleans Young Gen frequently and fast. Major/Full GC cleans Old Gen (slower, may stop the world).
Modern collectors: G1 (default since Java 9 - region-based), ZGC and Shenandoah (low-latency, sub-millisecond pauses).

questions.qText: What is a memory leak in Java? Common causes?

blocks.text: A memory leak is when objects are no longer needed but still referenced, so GC can't collect them. Common causes: 1) Static collections that keep growing. 2) Unclosed resources (connections, streams) - use try-with-resources. 3) Listeners and callbacks not removed. 4) Long-lived caches without eviction policy. 5) ThreadLocal not removed in thread pool scenarios. 6) Inner classes holding implicit references to outer class. Detection: heap dumps, profilers like VisualVM, JProfiler.

questions.qText: What are virtual threads in Java 21?

blocks.text: Virtual threads are lightweight threads managed by the JVM, not the OS. They have very low memory footprint - you can create millions of them. They map onto a small pool of OS threads (carrier threads). When a virtual thread blocks (I/O, sleep), it unmounts from the carrier thread, allowing the carrier to run another virtual thread. Huge benefit for I/O-bound applications like web servers. Use Thread.startVirtualThread() or Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor().

questions.qText: What is the difference between Java 8 and Java 17?

blocks.text: Java 8 (2014, LTS) introduced lambdas, streams, Optional, functional interfaces, new Date/Time API, default methods in interfaces.

Java 17 (2021, LTS) added: sealed classes (controlled inheritance), records (immutable data classes), pattern matching for instanceof, text blocks, switch expressions, enhanced pseudo-random generators, removed Applet API, strongly encapsulated JDK internals.
Other versions between (9-16) introduced: var, module system, HTTP client, jshell, helpful NPE messages, ZGC, Shenandoah.

questions.qText: What is the diamond problem and how does Java handle it?

blocks.text: Diamond problem: when a class inherits the same method from two paths, which one wins? Java doesn't allow multiple class inheritance to avoid this. But with multiple interface inheritance (Java 8+ default methods), the diamond can occur. Java handles it with three rules: 1) Class wins over interface. 2) More specific interface (subinterface) wins over its parent. 3) If still ambiguous, you MUST override the method in your class - compiler error otherwise.

questions.qText: What is a record class in Java?

blocks.text: Records (Java 16+) are immutable data carrier classes with minimal boilerplate. Syntax: 'public record Point(int x, int y) {}'.

The compiler auto-generates: private final fields, public constructor, accessor methods (x() and y()), equals(), hashCode(), toString(). All fields are final. Records can implement interfaces and have static methods and additional constructors, but cannot extend other classes.
Use case: DTOs, immutable value objects, response classes - any time you need a 'data bag' without writing 30 lines of boilerplate.

questions.qText: What is the difference between sealed and final class?

blocks.text: final class cannot be extended at all. sealed class (Java 17+) allows extension but only by classes explicitly listed in the 'permits' clause. Subclasses must be declared final, sealed, or non-sealed. Sealed classes give you the power of an inheritance hierarchy you can reason about completely - useful for pattern matching and modeling domain types.

questions.qText: What are pattern matching for instanceof and switch?

blocks.text: Pattern matching for instanceof (Java 16+): 'if (obj instanceof String s) { use(s); }' - automatically casts to String and binds variable s. No more redundant cast.

Pattern matching for switch (Java 21+): switch can match types and deconstruct records. Example: 'switch(shape) { case Circle c -> Math.PI * c.radius() * c.radius(); case Square s -> s.side() * s.side(); }'. Combined with sealed classes, the compiler can ensure all cases are covered (exhaustiveness check).

questions.qText: Difference between Callable and Runnable?

blocks.text: Runnable: run() returns void, cannot throw checked exceptions. Used for fire-and-forget tasks. Callable: call() returns a value of type V, can throw checked exceptions. Used when you need a result back. Callable is submitted to ExecutorService.submit() and returns a Future<V>, which you call .get() on to retrieve the result (blocks until done).

questions.qText: Explain the ExecutorService.

blocks.text: ExecutorService is a higher-level API for managing threads. Instead of manually creating Threads, you submit tasks (Runnable or Callable) to an ExecutorService, which manages a pool of threads. Factory methods in Executors: newFixedThreadPool(n) - fixed number of threads. newCachedThreadPool() - grows/shrinks as needed. newSingleThreadExecutor() - one thread. newScheduledThreadPool() - scheduled tasks. Always call shutdown() when done.

questions.qText: What is the difference between CompletableFuture and Future?

blocks.text: Future (Java 5): represents an async result, has get() to retrieve. Limited - no chaining, no callbacks, just blocking. CompletableFuture (Java 8): can be composed and chained. Supports callbacks (thenApply, thenAccept, thenCompose), combining (allOf, anyOf, thenCombine), exception handling (exceptionally, handle), and explicit completion. The modern way to do async in Java.

questions.qText: What is the difference between == and equals() for Integer wrapper?

blocks.text: Tricky question. == compares references. Integer cache exists for values -128 to 127 (autoboxing returns the same cached object), so Integer.valueOf(100) == Integer.valueOf(100) is true. But Integer.valueOf(200) == Integer.valueOf(200) is false! Always use .equals() for Integer comparison. This trap appears in real production bugs.

questions.qText: What does the 'transient' keyword do?

blocks.text: transient marks a field to be skipped during Serialization. When the object is serialized to bytes, transient fields are not included; on deserialization they get their default values (null, 0, false). Used for sensitive data (passwords), large derived fields you can recompute, or non-serializable types (like DB connections) inside a serializable class.

questions.qText: What is the difference between deep copy and shallow copy?

blocks.text: Shallow copy: copies the object's fields. For reference fields, both objects share the same referenced object. Modifying the inner object through one affects the other. Object.clone() does shallow by default. Deep copy: recursively copies all referenced objects too. Independent copies. Implement via custom logic, serialization, or copy constructors. Important when an object contains mutable nested objects.

questions.qText: Why are static methods not overridden, only hidden?

blocks.text: Static methods belong to the class, not instances. They are resolved at compile time based on the reference type, not the actual object (no dynamic dispatch). If a subclass declares a static method with the same signature, it 'hides' the parent's method - which one runs depends on the reference type used. This is called method hiding, not overriding. Real overriding requires runtime dispatch, which doesn't apply to statics.

questions.qText: What is the singleton design pattern? Best implementation in Java?

blocks.text: Singleton ensures only ONE instance of a class exists, with a global access point. Common uses: configuration, logging, connection pools, caches.

Best implementations: 1) Enum singleton (Joshua Bloch's recommendation) - thread-safe, serialization-safe, reflection-safe, simple. 2) Bill Pugh / static inner class - lazy, thread-safe via class loader guarantees. 3) Double-checked locking with volatile - works but more complex.
Pitfalls: hard to test, hidden global state, breaks with multiple class loaders. In Spring, beans are singletons by default, managed by the container.

questions.qText: Difference between git fetch and git pull?

blocks.text: git fetch downloads changes from the remote to your local repo but doesn't merge them. You can inspect them before merging. git pull = git fetch + git merge (or git rebase if configured). Pull immediately integrates changes. Best practice: fetch first, review, then merge or rebase intentionally.

questions.qText: Difference between merge and rebase?

blocks.text: Merge creates a new merge commit that ties together two branches. Preserves the actual history including parallel work.
Rebase replays your commits on top of another branch, creating a linear history. Looks cleaner but rewrites history.
Rule of thumb: rebase your local feature branch onto main before merging (clean history), but never rebase commits that have been pushed to a shared branch (others may have based work on them).

questions.qText: What is the difference between reset, revert, and checkout?

blocks.text: git reset moves the branch pointer backward, discarding commits. Soft keeps changes staged, mixed (default) keeps them unstaged, hard discards them. Dangerous on public branches.
git revert creates a NEW commit that undoes a previous commit. Safe on public branches because it doesn't rewrite history.
git checkout (legacy) and git restore (modern) restore files from a specific commit or branch.

questions.qText: How do you resolve a merge conflict?

blocks.text: 1) Git marks the conflicting files. Open them - you'll see '<<<<<<< HEAD', '=======', '>>>>>>> branch-name' markers.
2) Manually edit the file to keep the correct content (yours, theirs, or a mix). Remove all markers.
3) git add the resolved file. 4) git commit (during merge) or git rebase --continue (during rebase). Use 'git merge --abort' or 'git rebase --abort' to cancel.

questions.qText: What is git stash and when to use it?

blocks.text: git stash temporarily saves uncommitted changes (both staged and unstaged) so you can switch context. Use it when you're in the middle of work but need to quickly pull, switch branches, or do something else. Commands: 'git stash push -m "description"' to save, 'git stash list' to see all, 'git stash pop' to apply and remove latest, 'git stash apply' to apply but keep, 'git stash drop' to delete.

questions.qText: How to undo the last commit (multiple scenarios)?

blocks.text: If you haven't pushed: git reset --soft HEAD~1 (undo commit, keep changes staged), or git reset --mixed HEAD~1 (changes unstaged), or git reset --hard HEAD~1 (lose changes entirely).
If you have pushed and others have pulled: git revert HEAD - creates a new commit undoing the previous one.
To just change the message or add files: git commit --amend (only if not pushed).

questions.qText: Explain git rebase interactive.

blocks.text: git rebase -i HEAD~N opens an editor where you can manipulate the last N commits. Options: pick (keep), squash (combine with previous, edit message), fixup (combine, discard message), reword (change message), edit (pause to modify the commit), drop (delete). Used to clean up a feature branch before merging - squash 'WIP' commits, fix typos in messages, reorder commits.

questions.qText: What does git reflog do?

blocks.text: git reflog shows a log of where HEAD has been - every commit, reset, rebase, checkout. It's your safety net. If you accidentally delete a branch or hard-reset, the commits aren't immediately gone - you can find them in reflog and recover. Example: 'git reflog' shows 'a1b2c3d HEAD@{5}: commit: my work'. Then 'git checkout -b recovered a1b2c3d' brings it back. Reflog entries expire after 90 days by default.
Strong JS is non-negotiable for React. Don't skip this stage.
